A very light spray of oil on the basket itself, not on the nuggets, before adding them can help prevent sticking without affecting the crispness of the breading. If pieces do stick, let them sit for an extra minute after cooking finishes before trying to remove them, since they tend to release more easily once they've cooled slightly and the breading has fully set.

Cooking for a crowd or a family with picky eaters often means running multiple batches, which is where dual-zone or dual-basket air fryers become genuinely useful, letting you cook nuggets in one zone while fries or vegetables cook in the other, with both finishing around the same time. For smaller households, a compact single-basket model is often plenty for a couple of servings of nuggets without the bulk of a larger unit taking up counter space. If frozen nuggets, tenders, and similar kid-friendly foods are a regular part of your routine, basket size and shape make a real difference in how many batches you'll need to run on a busy weeknight. Our Air Fryers guide explains how capacity and wattage affect everyday cooking, and our comparison of top picks includes compact and larger options well suited to quick, kid-approved meals.

Pairing nuggets with sides is easy to do efficiently if your air fryer has a dual-zone design, since you can run nuggets in one basket while fries, tater tots, or vegetables cook in the other, often with a sync setting that times both zones to finish at the same moment. If you're using a single-basket model, a practical approach is to cook the side dish first, since most sides like fries or vegetables hold their crispness reasonably well for a few minutes, then cook the nuggets second since they're quick and best served immediately. Keeping cooked sides warm in a low oven, around 200°F (93°C), while the nuggets finish in the air fryer is a simple way to get everything to the table at roughly the same temperature without needing two appliances running simultaneously.

Dipping sauces are part of the experience for most people eating nuggets, and timing the sauce prep around the cook is worth a moment of planning. Because nuggets cook quickly, in the 10 to 12 minute range for standard sizes, there's a natural window during the halfway shake to quickly portion out ketchup, barbecue sauce, honey mustard, or ranch into small bowls, so everything is ready the moment the nuggets finish. If you want to warm a sauce like barbecue or buffalo slightly, a few seconds in the microwave while the nuggets are in their final minutes works well, though most dipping sauces are served cold or at room temperature and don't need any extra preparation beyond opening the bottle. Leftover cooked nuggets reheat surprisingly well too, often better than they did the first time around once the breading has had a chance to fully set. Reheat leftover nuggets at 375°F (190°C) for 3 to 4 minutes, which is usually enough to warm them through and re-crisp the breading without drying out the chicken inside, making them a solid option for next-day lunches packed straight from the fridge.

faq.question: What temperature should frozen chicken nuggets be cooked at?
faq.answer: Most frozen nuggets cook well at 400°F (200°C) for 10 to 12 minutes, with a shake at the halfway point for even browning.

faq.question: Do frozen chicken nuggets need to be thawed first?
faq.answer: No, nuggets should go directly from the freezer to the air fryer basket. Thawing first makes the breading soggier.

faq.question: How can I tell if air fryer nuggets are fully heated through?
faq.answer: Cut open the largest nugget in the batch or use an instant-read thermometer to confirm it reaches 165°F (74°C) at the center.

faq.question: Why do my chicken nuggets come out unevenly browned?
faq.answer: This is usually caused by overcrowding or skipping the halfway shake, which leaves pieces on the bottom of the basket more exposed to heat than those on top.

title: How to Cook Bacon in an Air Fryer
description: Cook bacon in an air fryer for crispy strips with less mess and no spattering grease, including timing for thin, regular, and thick-cut bacon.
author: Maya Hart
body: Bacon might be the single best argument for owning an air fryer if you've never tried it. Cooking bacon on the stovetop means standing over a spattering pan, dealing with grease splatter on your backsplash, and flipping strips constantly to avoid burning one side. The air fryer eliminates almost all of that. The hot air circulates around each strip, the fat renders and drips down into the bottom of the basket or onto the tray below, and you don't need to flip anything for thinner cuts. The result is bacon that's evenly crisp from edge to edge with a fraction of the cleanup. There's no need to preheat for bacon the way you would for fries or wings, since bacon releases its own fat as it heats and benefits from a slower temperature ramp that renders fat gradually rather than searing the outside before the fat has a chance to melt out. Lay strips in a single layer directly in the basket, with as little overlap as possible. Some overlap is unavoidable with longer strips in smaller baskets, but try to keep at least the centers separated so the strips don't fuse together as the fat renders and the bacon firms up.

For regular-cut bacon, cook at 350°F to 375°F (177°C to 190°C) for 8 to 12 minutes, depending on how crisp you like it and how many strips are in the basket. A single layer of 4 to 6 strips in a 5 to 6 quart basket typically takes around 10 minutes at 375°F (190°C) for a classic crisp-but-not-brittle texture. Thin-cut bacon cooks faster, usually 6 to 9 minutes at 375°F (190°C), and can go from perfectly done to burnt quickly in the last minute, so start checking at the 6-minute mark. Thick-cut bacon needs more time, typically 12 to 16 minutes at 360°F (182°C), since the extra thickness needs more time for the fat to render all the way through without the surface burning first.

Whether you need to flip bacon depends on your air fryer's design. In basket-style units where strips sit above a perforated tray, the hot air circulates underneath as well as above, so flipping is often unnecessary, though flipping once around the halfway point can help if you notice one side browning faster. In oven-style air fryers where bacon sits on a solid or lightly perforated tray closer to a single heating element, flipping halfway through produces more even results, since the side facing the element will brown noticeably faster than the side facing away.

Fat dripping into the bottom of the basket is normal and expected, but it can smoke if it accumulates and the air fryer runs hot, especially during longer cooks with thick-cut bacon or when cooking multiple batches back to back. If you notice smoke, it usually means fat has pooled and is making contact with the heating element area. Adding a small amount of water, about a tablespoon or two, to the bottom drawer beneath the basket before cooking can help prevent this by keeping the fat from smoking, and it makes cleanup easier since the fat solidifies in the water-diluted layer rather than baking onto the drawer.

Cooking bacon in batches is common since most baskets only fit a single layer of regular strips comfortably, usually 4 to 6 pieces depending on strip length and basket shape. If you're cooking for a larger group, you can stack a second batch right after the first, but empty and wipe out any accumulated grease between batches, both for safety and because a basket already coated in hot fat will cause the next batch to cook faster and potentially burn before you expect it to. Bacon ends, bacon bits for salads, or pre-cut bacon pieces cook faster than full strips because of their smaller size and increased surface area, typically needing just 6 to 8 minutes at 375°F (190°C) with a shake at the halfway point to keep pieces from clumping together as the fat renders. Turkey bacon and other leaner alternatives generally need slightly less time than pork bacon, around 7 to 10 minutes at 360°F (182°C), since they have less fat to render and can dry out if overcooked.

One advantage of air fryer bacon that's easy to overlook is how well it holds its shape for recipes where you want flat or gently curved strips, like for sandwiches or topping a burger, since the strips aren't being pushed around by tongs or a spatula the way they would be in a skillet. If you want perfectly flat strips for presentation, you can place a second perforated tray or a small oven-safe rack directly on top of the bacon to gently weigh it down during cooking, which prevents curling without affecting crispness.

Cleanup is genuinely one of the best parts of air fryer bacon. Once the basket has cooled slightly, most of the rendered fat can be poured off into a heatproof container for disposal or reuse in cooking, and the basket itself, along with any tray, can usually go straight into warm soapy water or the dishwasher if it's dishwasher safe. Compare that to scrubbing a cast iron skillet covered in baked-on bacon grease, and it's easy to see why bacon is one of the most-requested air fryer foods. If bacon is part of your regular breakfast rotation, basket size and tray design both affect how many strips you can cook at once and how easy cleanup will be. Larger baskets and XXL-style models can fit more strips per batch, which matters if you're cooking for a family. Take a look at our Air Fryers guide for details on capacity and tray accessories, and our comparison of top picks if you're considering an upgrade that handles bacon and other breakfast foods with less hassle.

Cooked bacon also reheats well in the air fryer if you're meal-prepping breakfast for the week. Store cooked strips in the refrigerator in a sealed container with a paper towel to absorb excess moisture, and reheat at 350°F (177°C) for 2 to 3 minutes, which restores most of the crispness without drying the strips out further. This makes bacon a good candidate for batch cooking on a weekend, since you can cook a large quantity, portion it out, and have crispy bacon ready in just a couple of minutes on busy mornings. If you're cooking bacon to crumble over salads, baked potatoes, or pasta dishes, cooking a larger batch ahead of time and storing it crumbled in the fridge or freezer saves time on the days you actually need it, and frozen bacon crumbles can go straight into hot dishes without thawing.

Beyond breakfast, cooked bacon is a useful ingredient to have on hand for a range of dishes, and the air fryer makes it practical to cook just a couple of strips at a time without firing up a full pan. A single strip or two cooked at 375°F (190°C) for about 8 minutes is enough for topping a burger, crumbling into a salad, or adding to a sandwich, and because the air fryer heats up almost instantly, there's much less overhead than heating a skillet for a small quantity. This also means bacon doesn't have to be an all-or-nothing breakfast commitment. You can cook exactly as much as a recipe calls for, whether that's two strips or a full pound, without much difference in effort per strip.

faq.question: Do I need to preheat the air fryer before cooking bacon?
faq.answer: No, bacon cooks well without preheating since it benefits from a gradual temperature ramp that renders the fat slowly before the surface crisps.

faq.question: How long does regular bacon take in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Regular-cut bacon typically takes 8 to 12 minutes at 350°F to 375°F (177°C to 190°C), while thin-cut bacon can be done in as little as 6 to 9 minutes.

faq.question: How do I stop my air fryer from smoking when I cook bacon?
faq.answer: Add a tablespoon or two of water to the drawer beneath the basket before cooking. This keeps rendered fat from pooling and smoking against hot surfaces.

faq.question: Should I flip bacon halfway through cooking in an air fryer?
faq.answer: It depends on the design. Basket-style air fryers with airflow on both sides often don't require flipping, while oven-style models with one heating element benefit from a halfway flip.

title: How to Reheat Pizza in an Air Fryer
description: Reheat pizza in an air fryer to get a crispy crust and melty cheese again, with timing tips for thin, thick, frozen, and stuffed-crust slices.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: Leftover pizza has two enemies: the microwave, which turns crust into something closer to a damp sponge while leaving the cheese either cold or rubbery, and the oven, which works fine but takes ten to fifteen minutes of preheating for what should be a two-minute job. The air fryer solves both problems. It heats up almost instantly, circulates hot air around the slice from multiple directions, and re-crisps the crust on the bottom while bringing the cheese back to a melty, just-bubbling state on top, all in about the time it takes to pour a drink.

No preheating is necessary for reheating pizza, which is one of the things that makes this method so convenient. Place one or two slices directly in the basket or on the tray, depending on your air fryer's shape, in a single layer without overlapping. Overlapping slices trap steam between them, which softens the crust exactly where you don't want it and can leave cheese sticking to the slice above it when you try to separate them. Set the temperature to 350°F to 375°F (177°C to 190°C) and reheat for 3 to 5 minutes, checking at the 3-minute mark. Thin-crust slices and slices with less topping reheat faster, often ready in 3 minutes at 350°F (177°C). Thicker slices, deep-dish, or slices loaded with extra toppings and cheese need closer to 5 minutes, sometimes at the higher end of 375°F (190°C) to make sure the center of the slice gets warm enough without the crust edges burning before the middle catches up.

You'll know pizza is ready when the cheese is visibly bubbling again and the crust, when you lift the slice slightly with tongs, feels firm and gives a slight crackling sound rather than bending limply. If the cheese is bubbling but the crust still feels soft, you can extend the cook by 1 minute at a time rather than adding several minutes at once, since pizza goes from perfectly reheated to overly crisp or burnt around the edges quickly once the cheese starts to brown further. Stuffed-crust pizza needs special attention because the cheese inside the crust takes longer to soften than the toppings on top need to reheat. For stuffed crust, start at a slightly lower temperature, around 325°F to 350°F (163°C to 177°C), for about 5 to 6 minutes, which gives the inner cheese more time to warm through gradually without scorching the outer crust before the center is done. If you're short on time and only care about the toppings being hot, the standard 350°F to 375°F (177°C to 190°C) for 3 to 5 minutes still works fine, just expect the stuffed crust itself to come out only partially warmed.

One trick that significantly improves results, especially for thinner crusts that tend to dry out, is placing a small oven-safe ramekin of water in the basket alongside the pizza, or using a humidity-retaining accessory if your air fryer has one. The steam released helps keep the crust from drying out completely while still allowing the bottom to crisp against the basket or tray. This isn't necessary for thicker or saucier slices, which generally have enough moisture of their own, but it can make a noticeable difference for plain cheese slices on thin crust.

If you're reheating a frozen leftover slice rather than one from the fridge, add an extra 1 to 2 minutes to the times above, and consider starting at a slightly lower temperature, around 325°F (163°C), for the first half of the cook to let the slice thaw evenly before increasing to 375°F (190°C) to crisp the crust. Going straight to high heat with a frozen slice often results in a hot, melted top with a still-cold center, since the heat doesn't have time to penetrate through the frozen layers before the surface is already done.

For larger gatherings or reheating a whole leftover pizza rather than individual slices, oven-style air fryers with multiple racks or larger basket capacities make this far more practical than reheating slice by slice. A pizza cut into wedges can be arranged across one or two racks, rotated halfway through if your unit has uneven heat distribution, and reheated in roughly the same 3 to 5 minute window per batch. Dual-zone models also let you reheat pizza in one zone while warming a side, like garlic bread or wings, in the other.

A common mistake is reheating pizza at too high a temperature for too long in an attempt to speed things up, which results in a crust that's hard and overly browned on the bottom while the cheese on top has gone past bubbling into a greasy, separated texture. Pizza reheats best with a gentle approach: moderate heat, a short cook time, and frequent checking rather than one long blast. Because slices vary so much in thickness, topping load, and how cold they were to start, your first reheated slice is the best guide for adjusting the next one. If reheating leftovers is something you do often, alongside cooking full meals, a versatile air fryer with both basket and oven-style functionality, or a model with enough capacity to handle a few slices at once, can make this kind of everyday task faster. Our Air Fryers guide covers the differences between basket and oven-style designs, and our comparison of top picks includes models that excel at quick reheating tasks as well as full cooking.

Different pizza styles respond differently to this method, which is worth knowing if you regularly order from more than one place. Thin, crispy-crust styles reheat almost perfectly, often coming out nearly indistinguishable from fresh. Deep-dish and pan-style pizzas with thicker, denser crusts take longer for the center to warm through, so these benefit most from the lower-temperature, longer-time approach used for stuffed crust. Pizza with a lot of fresh toppings, like leafy greens or fresh mozzarella added after baking, can dry out or become tough if reheated too long, so these are better served with a shorter reheat focused mainly on the crust and cheese, accepting that fresh toppings won't fully return to their original texture. Whatever the style, reheating one or two slices at a time rather than a whole pizza at once consistently gives the most even results, since it's easier for hot air to reach every part of a smaller load.

If you find yourself reheating pizza often enough that it's become a regular part of your routine, it's worth thinking about how your air fryer's basket size and shape accommodate slice shapes. Round baskets can make it awkward to fit wide triangular slices without folding or trimming, while square or rectangular baskets, common in larger and oven-style units, tend to fit standard pizza slices more naturally without needing to angle them. If pizza night leftovers are a frequent occurrence in your house, a slightly larger or rectangular basket shape can make this small but regular task noticeably more convenient, on top of whatever other cooking the unit handles. It's also worth keeping in mind that reheating pizza in an air fryer uses very little energy compared to preheating a full oven for the same task, which adds up if reheating leftover slices is something you do several times a week. The combination of speed, low energy use, and a result that's noticeably better than a microwave is part of why this has become one of the most commonly mentioned everyday uses for an air fryer once people own one, even among households that bought the appliance primarily for frying.

faq.question: What temperature is best for reheating pizza in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Most slices reheat well at 350°F to 375°F (177°C to 190°C) for 3 to 5 minutes, depending on crust thickness and topping amount.

faq.question: Do I need to preheat the air fryer to reheat pizza?
faq.answer: No, pizza can go directly into a cold basket. The air fryer heats up quickly enough that preheating isn't necessary for this short cook.

faq.question: How do I keep the crust from drying out when reheating pizza?
faq.answer: Place a small ramekin of water in the basket alongside the pizza, which adds steam and helps prevent thin crusts from becoming overly dry while still crisping the bottom.

faq.question: How long does frozen leftover pizza take to reheat in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Frozen slices generally need 1 to 2 minutes longer than fridge-cold slices, and starting at a lower temperature like 325°F (163°C) helps the center thaw evenly before crisping.

title: How to Cook Salmon in an Air Fryer
description: Cook salmon in an air fryer for moist, flaky fillets with crisp edges in under 12 minutes, with tips for skin-on, skinless, and frozen fillets.
author: Maya Hart
body: Salmon is one of those foods that goes from perfectly cooked to dry and chalky in the span of about a minute, which makes it a little intimidating for a lot of home cooks. The air fryer actually makes this easier to get right than a stovetop or oven, mainly because the cook times are short enough that you're never far from checking on it, and the circulating hot air cooks the fillet evenly from the top down while the bottom stays in contact with a hot surface, giving you a nicely set crust without needing to flip a delicate piece of fish. Start with fillets that are roughly the same thickness, since salmon cut from the thinner tail end will cook faster than a thick center-cut piece, and cooking them together means one will finish before the other. If your fillets vary noticeably in thickness, either trim them to similar sizes or plan to remove thinner pieces a minute or two early. Pat the fillets dry with a paper towel before seasoning, which helps any seasoning or oil adhere better and improves browning on the surface.

For skin-on fillets, place them skin-side down in the basket. Preheat the air fryer to 380°F (193°C) for about 3 minutes, then cook the salmon at 380°F (193°C) for 8 to 12 minutes depending on thickness. A standard 1-inch thick fillet usually takes about 10 minutes, while a thinner half-inch piece might be done closer to 7 to 8 minutes, and a thick center-cut piece over an inch and a half could need up to 12 minutes. There's no need to flip skin-on salmon. The skin crisps nicely against the basket while the flesh cooks gently from the circulating air above.

For skinless fillets, the same temperature and time ranges apply, but consider flipping once around the halfway point if you want even browning on both sides, since without skin to anchor it to the basket, the top and bottom can develop different textures. Skinless fillets also benefit from a light coat of oil before cooking, since they have less natural fat protection on the exterior compared to skin-on cuts, and the oil helps prevent the surface from drying out during the cook. The most reliable way to know when salmon is done is to check its internal temperature with an instant-read thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the fillet. The FDA recommends cooking fish to 145°F (63°C), at which point it will flake easily with a fork. However, many cooks prefer to pull salmon at 125°F to 130°F (52°C to 54°C) for a medium texture that's still slightly translucent and very moist in the center, since the fillet will continue cooking slightly from residual heat after it comes out of the basket. If you're cooking for anyone with a compromised immune system, pregnant, or otherwise needing fully cooked fish, stick to the 145°F (63°C) guideline.

Frozen salmon fillets can go straight into the air fryer without thawing, which is one of the more convenient aspects of cooking fish this way. Cook frozen fillets at 360°F (182°C) for about 7 minutes to gently thaw and begin cooking, then increase to 400°F (200°C) for another 5 to 8 minutes depending on thickness, until the internal temperature reaches your target. Pat any surface ice off frozen fillets before cooking if you have a moment, since a heavy frost layer can create excess steam in the basket during the first few minutes.

Seasoning salmon for the air fryer is straightforward since the cook time is short enough that most seasonings won't burn. A simple combination of salt, pepper, and a light brush of oil is enough to get great results, but glazes and marinades work well too. If using a sugar-based glaze like teriyaki, honey garlic, or maple, apply it during the last 2 to 3 minutes of cooking rather than at the start, since sugar can burn at the temperatures used for salmon if it's exposed to heat for the full cook time. A foil or parchment liner in the basket can help with sticky glazes, though leave gaps for airflow and don't fully enclose the fillet.

Other seafood follows similar logic with adjusted times. Shrimp, being much smaller, cooks in just 6 to 10 minutes at 380°F (193°C) and should be shaken once halfway through. Cod, halibut, and other white fish fillets of similar thickness to salmon need roughly the same 8 to 12 minutes at 380°F (193°C), though white fish can dry out a bit faster than salmon due to its lower fat content, so checking a minute or two early is a good habit. Scallops need just 5 to 7 minutes at 400°F (200°C) and benefit from a quick flip halfway for even color on both sides.

Basket size matters for fish more than you might expect, since fillets need space around them to cook evenly and a crowded basket leads to uneven doneness, with edges of touching fillets staying paler and softer than exposed surfaces. A single layer of two to three medium fillets fits comfortably in most 5 to 6 quart baskets, while dual-zone models let you cook salmon in one zone and a vegetable side in the other, finishing both at roughly the same time, which is especially convenient for a complete dinner without juggling a stovetop and oven. If seafood is a regular part of your meal planning, an air fryer with even heat distribution and enough basket space to avoid crowding fillets will make a noticeable difference in consistency. Our Air Fryers guide covers capacity and airflow considerations relevant to cooking fish, and our comparison of top picks highlights models that perform well for delicate proteins like salmon alongside everyday staples.

Leftover cooked salmon also reheats reasonably well in the air fryer, though gently is the key word. Reheat at 300°F (149°C) for about 3 to 4 minutes, just enough to take the chill off without pushing the fish past its original doneness, since salmon that was cooked to medium and then reheated at a high temperature for several minutes will likely end up overcooked the second time around. Cold leftover salmon also works well flaked into salads, grain bowls, or pasta without reheating at all, which avoids the texture changes that come with a second cook entirely. If you're meal-prepping salmon for the week, slightly undercooking it on the first pass, pulling it a few degrees below your target temperature, gives you a bit of a buffer for the inevitable reheating later without ending up dry.

Pairing salmon with a quick sauce or topping can elevate a simple weeknight fillet without much extra effort, and most of these can be prepared while the salmon cooks since the total time is so short. A simple lemon butter sauce, a spoonful of pesto, or a quick yogurt-based sauce with herbs and lemon all come together in the 8 to 12 minutes the salmon is in the basket. If you're cooking a full meal, this is also where dual-zone air fryers shine, since you can have salmon finishing in one zone while a quick vegetable side, like asparagus or broccoli, finishes in the other, both ready within a minute or two of each other without any extra coordination beyond starting them at slightly different times based on their respective cook times.

faq.question: What temperature should I cook salmon at in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Most fillets cook well at 380°F (193°C) for 8 to 12 minutes, depending on thickness, with no flipping needed for skin-on cuts.

faq.question: Do I need to thaw frozen salmon before air frying?
faq.answer: No, frozen fillets can go straight into the basket. Cook at 360°F (182°C) for about 7 minutes, then increase to 400°F (200°C) for 5 to 8 more minutes.

faq.question: What internal temperature should salmon reach?
faq.answer: The FDA recommends 145°F (63°C) for fully cooked salmon, though many cooks pull it at 125°F to 130°F (52°C to 54°C) for a more medium, moist texture.

faq.question: Should I flip salmon halfway through air frying?
faq.answer: Skin-on fillets don't need flipping since the skin crisps against the basket. Skinless fillets can be flipped once for more even browning on both sides.

title: How to Make Air Fryer Roasted Vegetables
description: Make perfectly roasted vegetables in an air fryer with golden, caramelized edges, covering timing for root vegetables, greens, and more produce.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: Roasting vegetables in an air fryer takes a fraction of the time that an oven does, and because the basket is smaller and the air circulates more directly around the food, you get caramelized, slightly charred edges that often take much longer to develop in a full-size oven. The tradeoff is that vegetables vary enormously in density, water content, and shape, so a single time and temperature doesn't work for everything. This guide breaks vegetables into rough categories based on how they behave in the basket, which makes it easier to combine different vegetables in the same cook or plan separate batches.

The general process is the same across categories. Cut vegetables into pieces of roughly similar size so they cook at the same rate, since a mix of large chunks and small pieces in the same batch means the small pieces will be done, and possibly burnt, well before the large ones finish. Toss the cut vegetables with a small amount of oil, just enough to lightly coat every piece, which helps with browning and prevents drying out, plus salt and any other seasonings. Spread everything in a single layer in the basket with as much space between pieces as you can manage, since vegetables release moisture as they cook and crowded pieces will steam each other rather than roast.

Dense root vegetables like potatoes, carrots, beets, and parsnips, cut into roughly three-quarter-inch cubes or wedges, need 18 to 22 minutes at 400°F (200°C), shaken every 7 to 8 minutes. These vegetables have enough structure to handle the higher temperature and longer time without falling apart, and the extended cook gives the natural sugars time to caramelize at the edges, which is where most of the flavor in roasted root vegetables comes from. Sweet potatoes follow similar timing but can brown faster due to their higher sugar content, so check at the 16-minute mark. Cruciferous vegetables, including broccoli, cauliflower, and Brussels sprouts, do best at 375°F (190°C) for 12 to 18 minutes depending on size. Broccoli and cauliflower florets, cut to roughly bite-size, need about 12 to 15 minutes, shaken at the halfway point, until the tips are browned and slightly crispy while the stems remain tender. Brussels sprouts, halved through the stem, need closer to 14 to 18 minutes, and cutting them in half rather than leaving them whole significantly improves both cooking speed and how well the cut sides caramelize against the basket.

Softer vegetables with higher water content, like zucchini, summer squash, bell peppers, mushrooms, and asparagus, cook fastest, typically 8 to 12 minutes at 375°F (190°C). These vegetables can go from perfectly tender with browned edges to mushy and watery quickly, since their high water content means there's less structure to hold them together once they soften. Check these closer to the 8-minute mark, especially for thinner cuts like asparagus spears or thinly sliced zucchini, which can be done in as little as 7 minutes. Onions, whether sliced into wedges or rings, need 12 to 16 minutes at 375°F (190°C) and benefit from a shake or stir partway through to prevent the outer layers from separating and burning before the inner layers soften. Whole garlic cloves, left in their skins, can be tossed in with other vegetables and roasted alongside them, becoming soft and spreadable after about 15 to 20 minutes, after which the skins slip off easily.

Mixing vegetables with different cook times in one batch works best when you stagger when each type goes into the basket. Start with the densest vegetables, like potatoes or carrots, and give them a head start of about 8 to 10 minutes before adding faster-cooking vegetables like zucchini or bell peppers to the same basket. This staggered approach takes a bit more attention than dumping everything in at once, but it produces a finished dish where every vegetable is properly cooked rather than a mix of mushy and undercooked pieces.

A few mistakes show up repeatedly with air fryer vegetables. Using too much oil leaves vegetables greasy and can cause excess smoking, since any oil that drips off pools at the bottom of the basket and continues to heat. Cutting pieces too small means they fall through basket holes or shrivel up before developing any real caramelization, while pieces cut too large won't cook through in a reasonable time. And skipping the shake or stir step, especially for vegetables like onions or peppers that release moisture as they cook, often results in steamed rather than roasted textures on the pieces that don't get redistributed.

For larger families or meal prep, batch size becomes the limiting factor with vegetables more than with most other foods, since a single layer is essential for proper roasting and vegetables take up more volume than you'd expect once cut. A larger basket, a multi-zone unit, or an oven-style air fryer with multiple racks lets you roast a full dinner's worth of vegetables without running three or four separate batches. If you regularly cook vegetable sides for a family, this is one of the areas where basket capacity makes the biggest practical difference in how long dinner takes to get on the table. If vegetables are a regular part of your weeknight cooking, choosing an air fryer with enough capacity to roast a full side dish in one batch, or a dual-zone model that lets vegetables and a protein cook together, can simplify dinner considerably. Our Air Fryers guide covers capacity considerations for batch cooking, and our comparison of top picks includes larger and multi-zone models well suited to roasting vegetables alongside the rest of your meal.

Leftover roasted vegetables reheat better in the air fryer than almost anywhere else, including the microwave, which tends to turn crispy edges soft and can make vegetables watery as they release moisture during reheating. To revive leftover roasted vegetables, spread them in a single layer and reheat at 375°F (190°C) for 3 to 5 minutes, which re-crisps the edges without needing any added oil. This makes roasted vegetables a good candidate for cooking in larger batches than you need for one meal, since the leftovers genuinely taste close to freshly made the next day rather than becoming a soggy afterthought. If you're meal-prepping for the week, slightly undercooking vegetables on the first pass, pulling them a couple of minutes early so they're tender but not yet fully caramelized, gives the reheat step room to finish the browning process without the vegetables turning mushy by the time you eat them.

Seasoning beyond salt and pepper is where roasted vegetables really come into their own, and the air fryer's quick cook time means you can experiment without committing to a long oven session. Garlic powder, smoked paprika, Italian seasoning, or a simple drizzle of balsamic vinegar added in the last few minutes of cooking all work well across most vegetable types. Parmesan cheese, sprinkled over vegetables during the final 2 to 3 minutes of cooking, melts and crisps slightly against the hot vegetables without burning, adding a savory finish that works particularly well with broccoli, cauliflower, and Brussels sprouts. Fresh herbs like parsley or basil are best added after cooking rather than before, since they can dry out and lose flavor and color if exposed to the full cook time.

faq.question: What temperature is best for roasting vegetables in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Dense root vegetables roast well at 400°F (200°C), while most other vegetables, including cruciferous and soft vegetables, do better at 375°F (190°C).

faq.question: Can I cook different vegetables together in the same batch?
faq.answer: Yes, but stagger the start times. Add denser vegetables like potatoes or carrots first, then add faster-cooking vegetables like zucchini or peppers about 8 to 10 minutes later.

faq.question: Why do my air fryer vegetables come out soggy instead of crispy?
faq.answer: This is usually from overcrowding the basket or using too much oil. Spread vegetables in a single layer with space between pieces and use just enough oil to lightly coat them.

faq.question: How often should I shake the basket when roasting vegetables?
faq.answer: Shaking every 7 to 8 minutes for denser vegetables, or once at the halfway point for faster-cooking vegetables, helps everything brown evenly.

title: How to Cook Steak in an Air Fryer
description: Cook steak in an air fryer for a flavorful crust and juicy interior, with timing for rare through well-done and tips for different steak cuts.
author: Maya Hart
body: Cooking steak in an air fryer surprises a lot of people the first time, mostly because they expect it to work like a microwave and just gently heat the meat through. In reality, a good air fryer running at 400°F (200°C) generates enough direct heat to develop a real crust on the outside of a steak, especially on cuts with some fat content that can render and baste the surface as it cooks. It won't replicate the deep char of a screaming-hot cast iron pan or a charcoal grill, but for a quick weeknight steak with minimal cleanup, it holds up better than most people expect.

Start with a steak that's been brought closer to room temperature, ideally resting out of the fridge for 20 to 30 minutes before cooking. A cold steak straight from the fridge will have a wider gap between the temperature of the exterior and the center by the time the outside develops color, making it harder to hit your target doneness without overcooking the outer layer. Pat the steak dry with paper towels, since any surface moisture will create steam in the basket and slow down browning. Season generously with salt and pepper, and a light coat of oil helps with browning, though steaks with a substantial fat cap or marbling, like ribeye, often don't need much added oil at all.

Preheat the air fryer to 400°F (200°C) for about 5 minutes. This step matters more for steak than almost anything else in this guide, because a hot basket gives the surface of the steak an immediate sear-like reaction the moment it goes in, which is where most of the flavor from browning develops. Place the steak in the basket without crowding, leaving space around the edges if you're cooking more than one, since steaks that touch will block airflow on those sides and develop pale spots where they're in contact with each other. For a 1-inch thick steak, cook at 400°F (200°C) and flip halfway through. For rare, around 120°F to 125°F (49°C to 52°C) internal, cook about 7 to 8 minutes total. For medium-rare, around 130°F to 135°F (54°C to 57°C), cook about 9 to 10 minutes. For medium, around 140°F to 145°F (60°C to 63°C), cook about 11 to 12 minutes. For well-done, above 160°F (71°C), expect 14 to 16 minutes, though well-done steaks cooked this way can dry out more easily, so consider a slightly lower temperature, around 380°F (193°C), for the back half of the cook to slow moisture loss.

Thickness changes these numbers significantly, more than almost any other variable. A thinner steak around half an inch thick will cook through in roughly half the time of a 1-inch steak at the same temperature, and can go from medium-rare to well-done in under a minute near the end of the cook, so check early and often. A thick-cut steak, an inch and a half or more, needs both more total time and benefits from a slightly lower temperature, around 380°F (193°C), for most of the cook before a final 2 to 3 minutes at 400°F (200°C) to finish the crust, which helps the inside catch up to the outside without the surface overcooking.

An instant-read thermometer is the only reliable way to hit a specific doneness consistently, since visual cues that work for grilling, like color and firmness, are harder to judge through an air fryer's basket. Insert the thermometer into the thickest part of the steak, avoiding any bone or large pockets of fat, which read differently from the meat itself. Pull the steak when it's about 5°F (3°C) below your target temperature, since the internal temperature will continue rising for a few minutes after cooking due to residual heat, a phenomenon known as carryover cooking.

Different cuts behave differently in the air fryer. Ribeye, with its higher fat content, tends to stay juicier even if slightly overcooked, and the rendering fat helps with browning. Leaner cuts like sirloin, filet mignon, or flank steak have less margin for error and can dry out if pushed past medium, so these benefit most from careful temperature monitoring. Thin cuts like skirt steak or flank steak cook very quickly, often in 6 to 8 minutes total at 400°F (200°C), and are easy to overcook if you walk away even briefly. Resting the steak after cooking is just as important with an air fryer as with any other cooking method. Move the steak to a cutting board or plate and let it rest for 5 minutes before slicing. This allows the juices, which have been pushed toward the center of the steak by the heat, to redistribute throughout the meat. Skipping this step means more juice ends up on the cutting board than in the steak itself, resulting in a drier bite even if the steak was cooked perfectly.

If you're cooking for more than one or two people, basket size becomes a real constraint with steak, since cramming multiple steaks into a small basket prevents proper air circulation and leads to uneven cooking between pieces. A larger basket or a dual-zone air fryer lets you cook two steaks side by side without crowding, or cook a steak in one zone while a side dish like vegetables or fries cooks in the other, timed to finish together. Oven-style air fryers with a wire rack can also accommodate larger cuts or multiple steaks more easily than a compact basket. If steak is something you cook regularly and want consistent results without guesswork, an air fryer with a powerful enough heating element to reach a true 400°F (200°C) and enough basket space to avoid crowding makes a meaningful difference. Our Air Fryers guide covers wattage and capacity considerations relevant to searing proteins, and our comparison of top picks includes models with the airflow and space to handle steak alongside the rest of your meal.

Marinades and dry rubs both work well with air fryer steak, though with some considerations. Wet marinades should be patted off before cooking, since excess liquid on the surface will create steam and prevent the kind of browning that gives steak its flavor, even though the marinade has already done its job of flavoring and tenderizing the interior. Dry rubs with a sugar component, similar to the concern with sugary glazes on salmon, can burn at 400°F (200°C) if applied too heavily, so a light hand with sugar-based rubs, or applying them only for the final few minutes, helps avoid a bitter, overly dark crust. Compound butter, a mixture of softened butter with herbs, garlic, or other flavorings, is one of the easiest ways to add richness to air fryer steak. Place a pat directly on top of the steak during the last minute of cooking or immediately after it comes out, letting it melt over the rested steak just before serving.

If you're cooking steak for a special occasion or simply want restaurant-quality results, a reverse sear approach can work well in an air fryer, particularly for thicker cuts. Start the steak at a lower temperature, around 325°F (163°C), for most of the cook until it's about 15°F (8°C) below your target internal temperature, then increase to 400°F (200°C) for the final 2 to 3 minutes to develop a deep crust. This two-stage approach gives you more control over the final crust without overcooking the interior, and it's especially useful for thick-cut steaks where getting both a good crust and an evenly cooked center in a single temperature pass can be difficult.

faq.question: What temperature should I use to cook steak in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Preheat to 400°F (200°C) for about 5 minutes, then cook the steak at the same temperature, flipping halfway through, adjusting time based on thickness and desired doneness.

faq.question: How long does a 1-inch steak take in an air fryer for medium-rare?
faq.answer: A 1-inch steak typically takes about 9 to 10 minutes total at 400°F (200°C) for medium-rare, around 130°F to 135°F (54°C to 57°C) internal, flipped halfway.

faq.question: Should I let steak rest after cooking it in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Yes, resting for about 5 minutes after cooking allows juices to redistribute through the meat, resulting in a juicier steak when sliced.

faq.question: Why does my air fryer steak come out unevenly cooked?
faq.answer: This is often due to uneven thickness, a cold basket from skipping preheating, or crowding multiple steaks together, which blocks airflow on the sides facing each other.

title: How to Bake in an Air Fryer
description: Learn how to bake in an air fryer, from cookies and muffins to quick breads and cakes, with temperature adjustments and pan recommendations.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: An air fryer is essentially a small, powerful convection oven, which means it can handle a surprising range of baking beyond fries and chicken, including cookies, muffins, banana bread, biscuits, and even small cakes. The main adjustments compared to baking in a full-size oven come down to two things: lower temperatures and shorter times, both because the air fryer's compact chamber and strong fan circulate heat much more aggressively than a standard oven, and because the heating element sits much closer to the food. The general rule of thumb when adapting a conventional oven recipe is to reduce the temperature by 25°F (about 15°C below the original Celsius temperature) and check for doneness at around 75 percent of the original baking time. So a recipe that calls for 350°F (177°C) for 20 minutes in a conventional oven would start around 325°F (163°C) and get its first doneness check around the 15-minute mark. This isn't an exact science, since every air fryer's fan strength and element placement differs slightly, but it's a reliable starting point that prevents the most common problem with air fryer baking, which is a baked good that's done or even burnt on the outside while still raw in the middle.

You'll need a pan or dish that fits inside your basket with room around the edges for air to circulate, since baked goods rely on that circulation for even cooking just as much as fried foods do. Small round or square metal cake pans, silicone molds, and oven-safe ramekins all work well, as do mini loaf pans for quick breads. Avoid filling any pan more than about two-thirds full, since baked goods rise during cooking and a too-full pan can overflow against the heating element, especially in air fryers where the element sits close to the top of the basket.

Cookies are one of the easiest things to bake in an air fryer and a great starting point if you're new to it. Drop spoonfuls of dough directly onto a piece of parchment paper cut to fit your basket, leaving at least an inch of space between cookies since they spread during baking, and bake at 320°F to 330°F (160°C to 166°C) for 6 to 9 minutes depending on size and thickness. Smaller, thinner cookies will be done closer to 6 minutes, while thicker, chunkier cookies need closer to 9. Check at the lower end first, since cookies can go from slightly underdone to overly browned on the bottom in just a minute or two given how close they sit to the heat source.

Muffins and cupcakes bake well in silicone muffin cups or a small metal muffin tin that fits your basket, typically at 320°F (160°C) for 12 to 18 minutes depending on size, checked with a toothpick inserted into the center, which should come out clean or with just a few moist crumbs. Mini muffins bake faster, often done in 10 to 12 minutes, while standard-size muffins need closer to 15 to 18 minutes. Because the top of an air fryer muffin is closer to the heating element than in a conventional oven, you may notice the tops brown more quickly than the centers fully set, so a slightly lower temperature with a longer time often produces better results than a hot, fast bake.

Quick breads like banana bread or zucchini bread require more patience, since their larger volume means the center takes longer to set than the outside, and it's easy to end up with a loaf that looks done but is gummy in the middle. Use a small loaf pan that fits your basket, bake at 310°F to 320°F (154°C to 160°C), and expect 35 to 50 minutes depending on the size of the loaf, checking with a toothpick starting around the 30-minute mark. If the top is browning too quickly before the center sets, loosely tent a piece of foil over the top of the loaf for the remaining bake time to slow further browning while the inside continues to cook.

Biscuits, scones, and other laminated or layered doughs do well at 350°F (177°C) for 8 to 12 minutes, and benefit from the air fryer's strong circulation, which can produce a flakier exterior than a conventional oven in some cases. Place these directly on parchment with space between pieces, similar to cookies, since they also expand during baking. Frozen biscuits from a can or the freezer section can go straight into the basket without thawing, typically needing 12 to 15 minutes at 330°F (166°C). Cakes are possible but require the most attention to pan size and temperature of anything in this guide, since a cake that's too tall for its pan relative to the basket's airflow will brown on top well before the center sets, similar to the muffin issue but more pronounced. A 6 to 7 inch round cake pan works in most standard baskets, baked at 310°F to 320°F (154°C to 160°C) for 25 to 35 minutes, checked with a toothpick. If you notice the top browning too fast, the foil-tenting trick used for quick breads applies here too, and can be applied partway through once the top has reached your desired color.

Oven-style air fryers with multiple racks have a real advantage for baking compared to compact basket models, since their larger interior allows for more even air circulation around pans and the option to bake on a middle rack farther from the top heating element, which more closely mimics a conventional oven's heat distribution. If baking is something you want to do regularly in an air fryer, an oven-style model with rack positions and a wider temperature range will generally give more consistent results than a small basket unit, which is better suited to smaller items like cookies and individual muffins. If you're interested in using your air fryer for baking on top of its everyday cooking duties, capacity and design make a real difference in what you can realistically bake and how evenly it turns out. Our Air Fryers guide covers the differences between basket and oven-style models relevant to baking, and our comparison of top picks highlights units with the interior space and temperature range to handle baked goods alongside typical air fryer meals.

Troubleshooting uneven baking is something almost everyone runs into at some point, and the fixes are usually simple once you know what's causing the problem. If the top of whatever you're baking is browning much faster than the inside is cooking, the most common culprits are a temperature that's too high for the size of the item, or a pan that's too tall relative to the basket, putting the top surface too close to the heating element. Lowering the temperature by another 10°F to 15°F (about 5°C to 8°C) and extending the time, or tenting foil over the top partway through, usually resolves this. If baked goods are coming out pale and underdone even after the recommended time, your air fryer may run cooler than average, in which case increasing the temperature by 10°F to 15°F (5°C to 8°C) for future bakes is a reasonable adjustment. Keeping notes on what worked for specific pans and recipes saves a lot of trial and error on repeat bakes.

faq.question: How do I convert a regular oven recipe for an air fryer?
faq.answer: Reduce the temperature by about 25°F (around 15°C) and check for doneness at roughly 75 percent of the original baking time, adjusting from there based on results.

faq.question: What kind of pans can I use for baking in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Small metal cake pans, silicone molds, oven-safe ramekins, and mini loaf pans all work well, as long as they leave room around the edges for air to circulate.

faq.question: Why does the top of my air fryer cake brown before the center is done?
faq.answer: The heating element sits close to the top of the basket, so tops brown faster than in a conventional oven. Lowering the temperature slightly or tenting foil over the top helps the center catch up.

faq.question: Are oven-style air fryers better for baking than basket models?
faq.answer: Generally yes, since their larger interior and rack positions allow more even heat distribution, which is helpful for cakes and quick breads, though basket models work fine for cookies and muffins.

title: Air Fryer Temperature Conversion Guide
description: Use this air fryer temperature conversion guide to adjust oven recipes correctly, with Fahrenheit-to-Celsius charts and time tips for any basket size.
author: Maya Hart
body: If you've ever pulled out a favorite recipe and wondered how to translate its conventional oven settings to your air fryer, you're not alone. Air fryers cook hotter and faster than a standard oven because the compact chamber circulates heated air directly around the food, so the same temperature setting produces a noticeably more intense cooking effect. The general rule of thumb that most home cooks rely on is to lower the oven temperature by 25 degrees Fahrenheit (about 15 degrees Celsius) and reduce the cooking time by roughly 20 percent when adapting a recipe for the air fryer. This isn't an exact science, but it's a reliable starting point that keeps food from burning on the outside while it's still raw in the middle.

To make this practical, here's how some common oven temperatures translate. A 350°F (175°C) oven setting becomes about 325°F (165°C) in the air fryer. A 400°F (205°C) setting drops to roughly 375°F (190°C). And a high-heat 425°F (220°C) recipe becomes closer to 400°F (205°C) in most baskets. These adjustments apply whether you're baking, roasting, or reheating, though baked goods like cakes and muffins are more sensitive to temperature swings than roasted vegetables or proteins, so it's worth erring on the lower end of the range for delicate batters.

Time is the other half of the equation, and it's just as important as temperature. A chicken breast that takes 30 minutes in a conventional oven at 375°F might only need 18 to 22 minutes at 350°F in the air fryer. Vegetables that roast for 35 minutes in the oven often finish in 15 to 18 minutes in the basket. Because the margin for error shrinks dramatically with faster cooking, check your food at the earliest point in the suggested range, then add time in small increments of two to three minutes rather than guessing at a longer block of time and risking overcooked results.

Every air fryer model runs slightly differently, even at the identical numerical setting. Compact basket models with smaller chambers, like a 2 to 4 quart unit, tend to run hotter relative to their dial setting because the heating element sits closer to the food. Larger dual-basket and oven-style air fryers, including multi-zone models, often distribute heat more evenly but may need a few extra minutes to come up to temperature initially. If you're new to a particular machine, run a simple test: cook a batch of frozen fries or a few chicken thighs at the recommended setting and note whether they finish faster or slower than expected, then adjust future recipes accordingly.

Preheating matters more in air fryer cooking than many people realize, especially when converting a recipe written for a conventional oven that assumes the cavity is already hot. Most air fryers reach their target temperature in three to five minutes, and starting your food in a fully preheated basket helps achieve the crisp exterior that makes air-fried food appealing in the first place. Skipping preheating is one of the most common reasons a converted recipe turns out soggy or pale, particularly with breaded items, frozen appetizers, or anything you want to develop a golden crust.

Altitude and ambient kitchen temperature can also nudge your results, though their effect is smaller than the basket-size and wattage differences between models. If you live at a higher elevation, foods may take slightly longer to cook through even though surfaces brown at the same rate, so rely on an instant-read thermometer for proteins rather than time alone. For baked goods, a toothpick test remains the most dependable way to confirm doneness regardless of what the timer says, since flour-based recipes are the most likely to look done on the outside while remaining underbaked inside.

When you're working from a recipe that was specifically written for an air fryer rather than converted from an oven version, you can generally trust the stated temperature and time as a starting point, but still treat the first attempt as a calibration run. Write down what worked, including any tweaks you made, directly on the recipe or in a notes app, so your second attempt benefits from what you learned. Over time you'll build a mental map of how your specific machine behaves across the temperature range, which makes future conversions almost automatic.

A digital probe thermometer is one of the most useful tools you can pair with an air fryer, especially while you're still learning how your unit's actual temperature compares to its display. Insert it into the thickest part of meat or poultry and you'll get a far more reliable doneness signal than any time chart can offer. For reference, chicken should reach 165°F (74°C), ground meats 160°F (71°C), and whole cuts of pork or beef typically 145°F (63°C) with a brief rest afterward. Relying on internal temperature rather than appearance alone removes most of the guesswork from converted recipes.

If you're shopping for a new air fryer and want one that makes temperature conversion less of a guessing game, look for models with finer temperature increments, accurate digital displays, and presets for common foods, since these give you a more consistent baseline to adjust from. Larger dual-zone baskets are also useful if you frequently cook multiple converted recipes at once with different temperature needs, since each zone can run independently. For a closer look at how different capacities and features affect everyday cooking, check out HomePilot's full air fryer guide and our roundup of top picks to find a model that fits the way you actually cook.

Cookbooks and recipe sites that publish dedicated air fryer charts can be a useful supplement once you understand the underlying logic, but treat any printed chart as an average across many different machines rather than a precise instruction for yours. Two air fryers from different brands set to the same 380°F can vary by 15 to 20 degrees in actual basket temperature, which is a meaningful difference for anything with a short cook time, like fish fillets or thin cuts of chicken. The most reliable way to build your own internal chart is to keep a small running log, even just a few notes in your phone, of the food, the setting you used, the actual time it took, and how it turned out. After a few weeks of regular use, you'll have a far more accurate reference for your specific unit than any general conversion table could provide.

It's also worth understanding why air fryers cook faster in the first place, since that context makes conversions feel less arbitrary. A conventional oven heats a large, mostly still volume of air, and food relies partly on radiant heat from the walls and partly on that air slowly transferring warmth. An air fryer's fan actively forces hot air directly across and around the food at high speed, which strips away the thin layer of cooler air that naturally forms around any object placed in an oven. That constant, forced contact is what allows lower temperatures to achieve browning and crisping that would otherwise require higher heat, and it's also why foods with a lot of surface area, like fries, wings, or vegetables, see the most dramatic time savings compared to dense, large items like a roast or a whole chicken, where the interior still has to heat through regardless of how fast the surface is exposed to hot air.

faq.question: What is the basic rule for converting oven temperatures to an air fryer?
faq.answer: Lower the oven temperature by about 25°F (15°C) and reduce the cooking time by roughly 20 percent as a starting point, then check your food early and adjust in small increments.

faq.question: Do all air fryers run at the same temperature for the same setting?
faq.answer: No. Smaller baskets often run hotter relative to their dial setting because the heating element is closer to the food, while larger or dual-zone models may need extra preheat time, so it's worth testing your specific unit.

faq.question: Why does my converted recipe come out soggy instead of crispy?
faq.answer: This usually happens when the basket isn't preheated before adding food. Preheating for three to five minutes helps create the crisp exterior that air frying is known for.

faq.question: Should I trust cooking time charts or a thermometer for doneness?
faq.answer: Use a thermometer whenever possible, especially for meat and poultry. Chicken should reach 165°F (74°C) and ground meats 160°F (71°C), since time charts vary by model and food thickness.

title: How to Cook Frozen Shrimp in an Air Fryer
description: Learn how to cook frozen shrimp in an air fryer for crispy, juicy results in under 15 minutes, with times, temps, and seasoning tips for any basket.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: Frozen shrimp is one of the best foods to keep stocked in your freezer for busy weeknights, and an air fryer turns it into a genuinely good meal in less time than it takes to boil pasta. There's no need to thaw shrimp before cooking, which is a major advantage over most other methods. Whether you're working with raw peeled and deveined shrimp, shell-on shrimp, or pre-breaded popcorn shrimp, the air fryer's circulating hot air thaws and cooks the shrimp simultaneously, giving you a slightly crisp exterior and tender interior without the rubbery texture that often results from microwaving or pan-frying straight from frozen.

For plain raw frozen shrimp, the easiest approach is to tip them directly into the basket without thawing. Set the air fryer to 380°F (193°C) and cook for 8 to 10 minutes total, pausing halfway through to shake the basket so the shrimp separate and cook evenly on all sides. Frozen shrimp tend to clump together straight out of the bag, so that mid-cook shake is important; if a few are still stuck together after the first few minutes, use tongs to pull them apart so the hot air can reach every surface. You'll know they're done when they turn opaque pink and curl into a loose C shape.

If you prefer to season your shrimp, the best approach is to add the seasoning after a brief 3 to 4 minute head start in the air fryer. This short initial cook lets the ice crystals melt off and the surface dry slightly, which helps oil and spices actually stick instead of sliding off into the basket. Once you've given them that head start, toss the shrimp with a teaspoon or two of oil along with garlic powder, smoked paprika, salt, and pepper, or whatever spice blend you like, then return them to the basket for the remaining cook time. This two-stage method consistently produces better-seasoned results than seasoning frozen shrimp straight away.

Pre-breaded frozen shrimp, including popcorn shrimp and butterfly-cut breaded shrimp, cook beautifully in an air fryer and come out far crispier than they do in a microwave. Arrange them in a single layer with a little space between each piece, since breaded items need airflow on all sides to crisp properly rather than steam. Cook at 400°F (205°C) for 9 to 12 minutes for larger butterfly shrimp, or 8 to 10 minutes for smaller popcorn-style pieces, shaking the basket once around the halfway mark. A light mist of cooking spray on the breading before cooking helps it brown more evenly, especially on pieces that land on top of the pile.

Shell-on frozen shrimp, sometimes sold head-on for shrimp boils or grilling, take a bit longer because the shell insulates the meat. Cook these at 380°F (193°C) for 12 to 15 minutes, shaking the basket two or three times during cooking to redistribute them. The shells will turn bright pink and slightly crisp at the edges when done. Peeling shrimp after air frying is actually easier than peeling them raw, since the cooked shell separates from the meat more cleanly, making this a good option if you want the flavor benefits of cooking with the shell on without the hassle of peeling beforehand.

One mistake that trips up a lot of people is overcrowding the basket, which is especially tempting with shrimp because individual pieces are small and it feels like you should be able to fit a pound in one layer. Shrimp cook fastest and crispest when they have room to breathe, so if you're cooking more than about three-quarters of a pound, work in two batches rather than piling everything in at once. Overcrowded shrimp tend to steam in the middle of the pile, coming out gray and rubbery instead of opaque and slightly firm, which is the texture you're aiming for.

Overcooking is the other major risk with shrimp, since they go from perfectly tender to tough and rubbery in a very narrow window, often just a minute or two. Start checking for doneness at the earlier end of any time range, especially if your shrimp are on the smaller side, since size varies considerably between bags labeled 'large,' 'jumbo,' or 'extra-large.' A properly cooked shrimp should be firm but still yield slightly when you press it, and the flesh should be opaque white or pink throughout with no translucent gray center.

Cooking times will vary somewhat depending on your air fryer's basket size and wattage, since a compact 2-quart basket sitting closer to the heating element will generally cook faster than a roomy 6-quart or dual-basket model. It's worth running your first batch a minute or two under the suggested time, checking doneness, and then noting how your particular machine performs so future batches are more predictable. This is especially useful if you cook frozen shrimp regularly, since once you've dialed in your model's timing, it becomes a near-effortless weeknight protein.

Once your shrimp are done, toss them with a squeeze of lemon, fresh herbs, or a quick sauce like garlic butter, cocktail sauce, or a spicy mayo for dipping. They work well over rice, in tacos, tossed through pasta, or piled onto a salad, and because the whole process takes under 15 minutes from freezer to plate, frozen shrimp in the air fryer is one of the most reliable last-minute dinner solutions you can have on hand.

If frozen shrimp and other quick seafood appetizers are a regular part of your routine, a dual-basket or larger single-basket air fryer gives you more flexibility to cook bigger batches without crowding, and a dual-zone model lets you cook shrimp in one basket while a side dish finishes in the other. Check the current Amazon or eBay listing for your model to confirm basket dimensions and any included accessories like racks that can help with batch cooking. For more guidance on choosing the right size and features for the way you cook, browse HomePilot's air fryer guide and our comparison of top picks.

Frozen shrimp comes in a range of sizes, typically labeled by a count-per-pound number such as 16/20, 21/25, or 31/40, and that number matters more for cook time than the marketing terms 'large,' 'jumbo,' or 'colossal,' which aren't standardized between brands. A bag labeled 21/25 means there are roughly 21 to 25 shrimp per pound, so smaller numbers mean bigger shrimp and longer cook times, while larger numbers mean smaller shrimp that cook faster and need closer watching. If you're used to cooking one size and switch to a different bag, expect to adjust your time by a minute or two in either direction, and when in doubt, pull a piece out and cut it in half to check that the center is opaque rather than relying on color alone, since the surface can look done before the very center has finished cooking.

Beyond the basic preparations already covered, frozen shrimp pairs well with quick pan sauces you can make while the shrimp cooks, since the air fryer frees up your stovetop entirely. While the basket runs its 8 to 10 minute cycle, you have just enough time to melt butter with garlic and a squeeze of lemon for a simple scampi-style sauce, or whisk together a quick honey-sriracha glaze to toss the shrimp in once they come out. This kind of parallel cooking is one of the underrated advantages of using an air fryer for proteins like shrimp: because the appliance handles the protein hands-off, your stovetop and your attention are both free for sauces, sides, or simply getting the table set, which is part of why frozen shrimp in the air fryer works so well as an actual weeknight dinner rather than just a snack or appetizer.

faq.question: Do I need to thaw shrimp before air frying?
faq.answer: No, you can cook shrimp directly from frozen. The air fryer's hot circulating air thaws and cooks the shrimp at the same time, usually in 8 to 15 minutes depending on the type.

faq.question: What temperature should I use for frozen shrimp in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Plain raw shrimp do well at 380°F (193°C) for 8 to 10 minutes, while breaded shrimp benefit from a slightly higher 400°F (205°C) for 8 to 12 minutes, with a basket shake halfway through.

faq.question: How do I get seasoning to stick to frozen shrimp?
faq.answer: Give the shrimp a 3 to 4 minute head start in the air fryer to melt the ice and dry the surface, then toss with oil and seasoning before returning them to finish cooking.

faq.question: How do I know when air-fried shrimp are done?
faq.answer: Shrimp are done when they turn opaque white or pink, curl into a loose C shape, and feel firm but still slightly yielding when pressed. A tight, tense curl usually means they're overcooked.

title: How to Make Homemade French Fries in an Air Fryer
description: Make crispy homemade french fries in an air fryer with this step-by-step guide covering soaking, oil ratios, temperatures, and double-frying technique.
author: Maya Hart
body: Making french fries from scratch in an air fryer takes a bit more effort than dumping in a bag of frozen fries, but the payoff is worth it: you control the cut, the seasoning, and the level of crispness, and the result genuinely rivals deep-fried fries with a fraction of the oil. The process starts with the right potato. Russet potatoes are the standard choice for fries because their high starch and low moisture content gives you that fluffy interior and crisp shell. Yukon Golds work too and produce a slightly creamier interior, but avoid waxy potatoes like red or new potatoes, which hold too much moisture and tend to come out soft rather than crisp.

Cut your potatoes into even sticks, somewhere between a quarter and a third of an inch thick, since uniform sizing is the single biggest factor in getting fries that cook at the same rate. Thinner fries, closer to shoestring width, cook faster and crisp up more easily but can dry out if left too long, while thicker steak fries take longer and have a softer center. Whatever thickness you choose, try to keep your cuts consistent across the whole batch so you're not pulling some out done while others are still pale and limp.

The soaking step is the part most people skip, and it's the one that makes the biggest difference. After cutting your potatoes, submerge them in a bowl of cold water for at least 30 minutes, or up to a few hours in the refrigerator if you want to prep ahead. This soak pulls excess surface starch out of the potatoes, which is what causes fries to stick together and turn gummy instead of crisp. After soaking, drain the potatoes and pat them completely dry with a clean kitchen towel or paper towels; any remaining surface moisture will create steam in the basket and prevent browning.

Once dry, toss the fries with oil, but use less than you might think. About one to one and a half tablespoons of oil per pound of potatoes is plenty, since the air fryer's circulating heat does most of the crisping work and excess oil just pools in the bottom of the basket and can cause smoking. Neutral oils with a high smoke point, like avocado, canola, or vegetable oil, work best. Toss the potatoes in a bowl rather than directly in the basket to make sure every piece gets an even, light coating before they go in to cook.

Preheat your air fryer to 380°F (193°C) for about three minutes before adding the fries. Spread them in a single layer, and don't be afraid to leave noticeable gaps between pieces. This is the step where overcrowding does the most damage to fries specifically, because fries that touch each other steam along those contact points and end up with soft, pale patches. If you've cut a full pound or more of potatoes, plan on cooking in two batches rather than trying to fit everything in at once, especially in a compact basket.

Cook the fries for 15 to 20 minutes total, shaking or tossing the basket every 5 minutes to redistribute the pieces and expose different sides to the heating element. Around the 15-minute mark, check a few fries from different parts of the basket; thinner shoestring cuts may be done closer to 15 minutes, while thicker steak-cut fries can take the full 20 or even a few minutes longer. You're looking for a deep golden color and a firm, crisp shell when you bite into one, with a soft, fluffy interior.

For an extra-crispy result that more closely mimics double-fried restaurant fries, try a two-stage cook. First, cook the fries at 325°F (165°C) for about 10 minutes, which gently cooks the interior through without much browning. Remove them, let them rest for a few minutes, then increase the temperature to 400°F (205°C) and cook for another 8 to 10 minutes until deeply golden and crisp. This two-temperature method produces a noticeably crunchier exterior than a single cook at one temperature, and it's the technique to use if you've ever been disappointed by fries that are crisp on the outside but mushy just underneath.

Season your fries immediately after they come out of the basket, while they're still hot, since salt and other seasonings adhere much better to hot, slightly oily fries than to ones that have cooled. A simple combination of salt and a touch of garlic powder or smoked paprika works well, or try tossing them with grated parmesan and chopped herbs for a more elevated side dish. If you're making a large batch across multiple rounds, keep finished fries warm in a low oven, around 200°F (93°C), rather than piling them in a covered bowl, which traps steam and softens the crust you worked to build.

A few common mistakes are worth calling out specifically. Skipping the soak is the most frequent culprit behind gummy fries. Using too much oil leads to greasy, soggy results and excess smoke. And cutting fries unevenly means some pieces finish minutes before others, forcing you to choose between underdone and overdone. If your fries consistently come out pale even after the full cook time, your air fryer may be running cooler than its display suggests, so try increasing the temperature by 15 to 20 degrees on your next batch and see if that closes the gap.

Cooking times and capacity matter a lot here, since fries take up more basket space than most foods and benefit from a model with good airflow and a wide, shallow basket shape that maximizes single-layer surface area. If you make fries often, a larger basket or dual-zone air fryer can cut down on the number of batches needed for a family-sized portion. Browse HomePilot's air fryer guide and our comparison of top picks to find a model with the capacity and features that suit how often you fry.

Sweet potato fries deserve a separate mention because they behave differently from russet potatoes due to their higher sugar content, which makes them prone to burning before they crisp. Cut sweet potato fries slightly thicker than you would russets, closer to a third to half an inch, and skip or shorten the soaking step since sweet potatoes hold less surface starch. Cook at 380°F (193°C) for 15 to 18 minutes, shaking every 5 minutes, and watch closely in the last few minutes since the natural sugars caramelize quickly and can go from perfectly browned to scorched in under a minute. A light dusting of cornstarch before tossing with oil can help sweet potato fries achieve a crisper exterior, since their higher moisture content otherwise tends to keep them on the softer side even with good technique.

If you're feeding a crowd or batch-cooking fries for the week, you can par-cook a large quantity at the lower 325°F (165°C) stage, let the fries cool completely, and refrigerate them for up to a couple of days before finishing them at 400°F (205°C) for the final crisping stage. This effectively splits the two-stage method across separate cooking sessions, which is useful if you're prepping for a gathering and don't want to be tied to the air fryer right before serving. Just make sure the par-cooked fries are fully cooled and stored in a single layer if possible, since stacking warm fries traps steam and can make them gummy by the time you're ready for the second stage.

faq.question: Do I need to soak potatoes before air frying french fries?
faq.answer: Yes, soaking cut potatoes in cold water for at least 30 minutes removes excess surface starch, which prevents the fries from sticking together and helps them crisp instead of turning gummy.

faq.question: How much oil should I use for homemade air fryer fries?
faq.answer: About one to one and a half tablespoons of oil per pound of potatoes is enough. Too much oil causes greasy results and excess smoke, since the air fryer does most of the crisping with hot air.

faq.question: What temperature and time work best for homemade fries?
faq.answer: Cook at 380°F (193°C) for 15 to 20 minutes, shaking the basket every 5 minutes. For extra crispiness, try 325°F (165°C) for 10 minutes followed by 400°F (205°C) for 8 to 10 minutes.

faq.question: Why do my homemade fries come out soft instead of crispy?
faq.answer: The most common causes are skipping the soaking step, not drying the potatoes thoroughly before adding oil, or overcrowding the basket so the fries steam instead of crisp.
