title: How to Cook a Whole Chicken in an Air Fryer
description: Cook a whole chicken in an air fryer with crispy skin and juicy meat using this guide covering sizing, seasoning, temperature, and resting times.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: A whole roast chicken is one of the most satisfying things you can make in an air fryer, producing crackling, deeply browned skin and tender meat in roughly half the time of a conventional oven, with far less heat thrown into your kitchen. The first thing to check before you start is whether your chicken will actually fit. Most basket-style air fryers can accommodate a bird in the 3 to 4.5 pound range, while larger oven-style or extra-large basket models can handle a 5 to 6 pound chicken. Weigh your bird and measure your basket's interior height and width before you commit, since a chicken that's too large to allow air to circulate around it won't cook evenly and may not crisp properly on the sides.

Start by patting the chicken completely dry, inside and out, with paper towels. This step matters more for a whole bird than almost anything else you'll do, because any surface moisture turns to steam in the basket and prevents the skin from crisping. If you have time, an even better approach is to leave the chicken uncovered in the refrigerator for a few hours or overnight before cooking, which dries the skin further and gives you noticeably crispier results, similar to the dry-brining technique used for oven roasting.

Once the chicken is dry, season it generously. A simple combination of salt, pepper, garlic powder, and paprika rubbed both under and over the skin gives you a flavorful, golden-brown finish, but feel free to use whatever herb blend you prefer. Rubbing a thin layer of oil or softened butter over the skin before seasoning helps the spices stick and promotes browning. If you want to add aromatics, tuck a halved lemon, a few garlic cloves, or fresh herb sprigs into the cavity, which will perfume the meat from the inside as it cooks without adding much bulk to the bird.

Truss the chicken if you can, tying the legs together with kitchen twine and tucking the wing tips behind the back. This isn't strictly necessary, but it helps the bird hold a more compact, even shape, which matters in the confined space of an air fryer basket where loose limbs can brush against the heating element and burn before the rest of the bird is done. If your air fryer comes with a roasting rack or trivet, use it; elevating the chicken off the bottom of the basket allows hot air to circulate underneath and helps the skin crisp on all sides rather than just the top.

Place the chicken breast-side down in the basket to start. Cook at 350°F (175°C) for about 30 minutes, then carefully flip the chicken breast-side up using two sturdy utensils or silicone-tipped tongs, taking care not to tear the skin. Continue cooking at the same temperature for another 25 to 35 minutes, depending on the size of the bird. A 3.5 to 4 pound chicken typically needs 55 to 65 minutes total, while a larger 5 to 6 pound bird in an extra-large unit may need closer to 70 to 80 minutes. These times are a starting point and will vary based on your specific air fryer's wattage and basket size.

The only reliable way to confirm a whole chicken is done is with an instant-read thermometer. Insert it into the thickest part of the thigh, avoiding the bone, and look for a reading of 165°F (74°C). Check the breast as well, since it can occasionally cook faster than the thigh depending on positioning. If the skin is browning too quickly before the interior reaches temperature, which can happen in smaller, hotter baskets, loosely tent the top of the chicken with a small piece of foil for the remaining cook time to slow surface browning without stopping the interior cooking.

Once the chicken reaches temperature, remove it from the basket and let it rest for 10 to 15 minutes before carving. This resting period allows the juices, which get pushed toward the center of the meat during cooking, to redistribute throughout the bird. Skipping this step is one of the most common reasons a perfectly cooked chicken ends up dry on the cutting board, since carving immediately lets those juices run out onto the board instead of staying in the meat.

If you're cooking for a smaller household and a whole chicken feels like overkill, the same techniques apply to chicken pieces or a spatchcocked bird, which lies flatter and cooks more quickly and evenly because there's no thick cavity insulating the breast meat. To spatchcock, remove the backbone with kitchen shears and press the chicken flat; this configuration often fits better in basket-style fryers and can cook in 35 to 45 minutes at 375°F (190°C), with no flipping required since the whole top surface is exposed to the heating element.

Don't discard the drippings that collect in the bottom of the basket or drawer. They're packed with rendered fat and flavor and make an excellent base for a quick pan gravy: just pour them into a small saucepan, whisk in a bit of flour or cornstarch slurry, add a splash of stock, and simmer until thickened. This is a small touch that turns an air-fried chicken dinner into something that feels like a proper Sunday roast with very little extra effort.

Whole chicken is one of the best tests of an air fryer's real-world capacity, since it requires enough vertical and horizontal clearance for air to circulate around a bulky item. If whole birds are a regular part of your cooking, look for a model marketed with extra-large or XXL capacity, ideally with a basket depth of at least 5 inches and a width that comfortably fits a 5 to 6 pound chicken with room to spare. For help comparing capacities and features across models, take a look at HomePilot's air fryer guide and our roundup of top picks.

Carving a whole chicken straight out of an air fryer basket is a little different from carving one off a roasting pan, mainly because there's less room to maneuver and the basket itself can be awkward to work around. The easiest approach is to lift the chicken onto a cutting board immediately after its rest, using two utensils or a sturdy pair of tongs supporting it from both ends so it doesn't fall apart on the way out, since air-fried chicken tends to be so tender that the legs and wings can separate from the body with very little resistance. Once it's on the board, standard carving technique applies: remove the legs and thighs first by cutting through the joint where they meet the body, then slice the breast meat against the grain in even pieces. Carving on a board with a juice groove, or simply tilting the board slightly, helps capture any remaining juices that you can spoon back over the sliced meat.

Leftover roast chicken from an air fryer reheats and repurposes well, which makes cooking a whole bird worthwhile even for smaller households since you'll likely have meat left over for a second meal. Shredded leftover chicken can go into soups, salads, sandwiches, or quesadillas, and the crispy skin, if any remains, can be chopped and crisped further in a quick 3 to 4 minute air fryer run at 380°F (193°C) to use as a flavorful topping for salads or baked potatoes rather than discarding it. Store leftover chicken in an airtight container for up to three to four days, and if you're planning to reheat slices later, a brief few minutes back in the air fryer at 350°F (175°C) restores some of the crispness to the skin far better than a microwave would.

faq.question: What size chicken fits in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Most basket-style air fryers handle a 3 to 4.5 pound chicken comfortably, while extra-large or XXL models can fit a 5 to 6 pound bird. Always check your basket's interior dimensions before buying a chicken.

faq.question: How long does it take to cook a whole chicken in an air fryer?
faq.answer: A 3.5 to 4 pound chicken typically takes 55 to 65 minutes at 350°F (175°C), flipping breast-side up halfway through. Larger birds in bigger units may need 70 to 80 minutes.

faq.question: Should I cook a whole chicken breast-side up or down?
faq.answer: Start breast-side down for the first half of cooking to protect the breast meat, then flip it breast-side up for the remainder so the skin can crisp and brown evenly.

faq.question: How do I know when a whole air-fried chicken is done?
faq.answer: Use an instant-read thermometer in the thickest part of the thigh, avoiding the bone. It should read 165°F (74°C). Check the breast too, since it can sometimes finish first.

title: How to Air Fry Tofu
description: Get crispy air fryer tofu every time with this guide on pressing, marinating, cornstarch coating, temperature, and timing for any basket size.
author: Maya Hart
body: Tofu has a reputation for being bland and spongy, but most of that comes down to how it's prepared rather than the tofu itself, and an air fryer is genuinely one of the best tools for turning a block of tofu into something with a crisp, golden exterior and a chewy, satisfying bite. The process has a few key steps that make a real difference, and skipping any of them is usually why air-fried tofu turns out soft or watery instead of crisp. The good news is that none of these steps are difficult, they just take a little planning ahead of your actual cooking time.

Start with the right tofu. Extra-firm or firm tofu are the only types worth air frying; silken and soft tofu contain too much water and will fall apart in the basket. Once you've got a block of extra-firm tofu, the next step is pressing out as much of its packing liquid as possible. Wrap the block in a clean kitchen towel or several layers of paper towels, place it on a plate, and set something heavy on top, like a cast iron pan or a few stacked cans. Press for at least 20 to 30 minutes; the longer you press, within reason, the crispier your final result will be, since every bit of moisture you remove now is moisture that won't turn to steam in the basket later.

After pressing, cut the tofu into your desired shape. Cubes around three-quarters of an inch to one inch are the most common and give you a good ratio of crisp surface area to soft interior. Slabs work well if you're making something like a tofu 'steak,' and smaller cubes around half an inch crisp up faster but can dry out if overcooked. Whatever shape you choose, try to keep the pieces uniform so they finish cooking around the same time, just as you would with vegetables or potatoes.

If you're marinating the tofu, do it after pressing and cutting, and give it at least 15 to 30 minutes, though tofu can also sit in a marinade for several hours in the refrigerator if you want deeper flavor. A simple soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger marinade works well, but keep in mind that wetter marinades can interfere with crisping. If maximum crunch is your priority, pat the marinated tofu dry with a paper towel before adding any coating, removing the surface liquid while leaving the absorbed flavor behind.

The single biggest upgrade you can make to air-fried tofu is a light cornstarch coating. After pressing, cutting, and optionally marinating and patting dry, toss the tofu cubes in about a tablespoon of cornstarch per block of tofu, making sure each piece gets a thin, even dusting. Cornstarch creates a delicate crust as it cooks that turns noticeably crispier and crunchier than tofu cooked without any coating, and it's a trick borrowed from restaurant-style crispy tofu that translates perfectly to the air fryer. A light spray or toss of oil after the cornstarch helps it brown evenly.

Preheat your air fryer to 400°F (205°C) for a few minutes, then arrange the tofu cubes in a single layer with space between each piece. This spacing is especially important for tofu because the cornstarch coating needs direct airflow to crisp; pieces that touch each other will steam along those edges and stay soft. Cook for 15 to 20 minutes total, shaking or flipping the basket every 5 to 7 minutes so all sides get exposed to the heat. You're looking for a deep golden-brown color and a firm, slightly crackly exterior when you tap a piece with a fork.

If your tofu comes out unevenly browned, with some pieces golden and others still pale, it's almost always a spacing or batch-size issue. Tofu cubes that end up underneath or sandwiched between other pieces simply don't get enough hot air contact. If you're cooking a full block's worth of cubes and your basket is on the smaller side, split the batch in half and cook in two rounds rather than crowding everything in at once; the extra few minutes is worth the consistency.

Tofu is also one of the more forgiving foods to reheat in an air fryer, which makes it a great meal-prep option. Cook a large batch, store it in the refrigerator, and when you're ready to eat, give it 4 to 6 minutes at 380°F (193°C) to re-crisp the exterior, which works far better than microwaving, which tends to make leftover tofu rubbery. This makes air-fried tofu a practical option for building bowls, salads, or stir-fries throughout the week without sacrificing texture on day three or four.

Beyond cubes, you can use the same pressing-and-cornstarch approach for tofu nuggets, sticks, or even crumbled tofu seasoned to mimic a ground meat texture for tacos or scrambles. Crumbled tofu cooks faster, generally 10 to 12 minutes at 380°F (193°C) with a stir halfway through, and develops crispy edges that add texture to whatever dish you're building. The same general principles, pressing out moisture and using a light starch coating, apply across all of these variations.

Because tofu benefits so much from single-layer spacing, a wider basket or a model with a flat, shallow tray gives you better results in fewer batches than a deep, narrow basket where pieces tend to pile up. If tofu and other plant-based proteins are a regular part of your meals, a dual-zone or larger-capacity air fryer lets you cook a full batch alongside a side of vegetables or rice without crowding either. For help finding a model with the right basket shape and capacity for your cooking style, check out HomePilot's air fryer guide and our comparison of top picks.

Beyond cornstarch, a few other coatings and techniques are worth experimenting with depending on the dish you're building. A mixture of cornstarch and a small amount of nutritional yeast or finely grated parmesan adds a savory, slightly cheesy crust that works particularly well on tofu destined for pasta dishes or as a salad topping. For an Asian-inspired crispy tofu, a thin coating of rice flour instead of cornstarch produces an even more delicate, shatter-like crust, though rice flour can brown a little faster, so keep an eye on it in the last few minutes. Whatever coating you choose, the underlying principles stay the same: press out moisture first, apply the coating in a thin even layer, and give the pieces room to breathe in the basket.

Tofu marinades and sauces also interact with the air fryer in ways worth knowing about. Sugary marinades, like a teriyaki-style sauce with honey or brown sugar, can burn at high temperatures before the tofu itself is fully crisped, so if you're using a sweet marinade, either apply it as a glaze during the last 3 to 5 minutes of cooking rather than marinating beforehand, or drop your cooking temperature to around 375°F (190°C) and extend the time slightly to compensate. This same principle applies to other proteins too, but it's especially relevant for tofu since its neutral flavor often leads cooks to reach for bolder, sweeter sauces that need a bit of extra care under an air fryer's concentrated heat.

faq.question: Do I need to press tofu before air frying it?
faq.answer: Yes, pressing extra-firm tofu for at least 20 to 30 minutes removes excess water that would otherwise turn to steam in the basket and prevent the tofu from getting crispy.

faq.question: What's the best coating for crispy air fryer tofu?
faq.answer: A light dusting of cornstarch, about a tablespoon per block, creates a delicate crust that crisps up much more than uncoated tofu. A light spray of oil after coating helps it brown evenly.

faq.question: What temperature and time should I use for air fryer tofu?
faq.answer: Cook pressed, cornstarch-coated tofu cubes at 400°F (205°C) for 15 to 20 minutes, shaking or flipping the basket every 5 to 7 minutes until golden brown and firm.

faq.question: Can I reheat leftover air-fried tofu and keep it crispy?
faq.answer: Yes, reheating leftover tofu at 380°F (193°C) for 4 to 6 minutes re-crisps the exterior much better than a microwave, which tends to make tofu rubbery.

title: Air Fryer Breakfast Recipes
description: Discover air fryer breakfast recipes for bacon, eggs, hash browns, pastries, and more, with exact times and temps for a faster morning routine.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: Mornings are usually the worst time to deal with multiple pans, splattering grease, and a sink full of dishes before you've even had coffee, which is exactly why an air fryer earns its keep at breakfast. It cooks bacon without the splatter, crisps hash browns without flipping them in oil, and can handle pastries, eggs, and even French toast sticks, often while you're getting ready elsewhere in the house. Below are some of the most useful breakfast applications, along with the times and temperatures that work across most basket sizes, with the understanding that your specific model may run a little faster or slower.

Bacon is the breakfast food that benefits most dramatically from the air fryer. Lay strips in a single layer, slightly overlapping is fine since bacon shrinks as it cooks, and cook at 380°F (193°C) for 8 to 12 minutes depending on thickness and how crispy you like it. There's no need to flip thinner bacon, though thicker-cut slices benefit from a flip around the 6-minute mark. The grease drips into the bottom of the basket or drawer rather than splattering across your stovetop, and cleanup is just wiping out the drawer once it cools. Check at the lower end of the time range first, since bacon can go from chewy to burnt quickly once it's mostly rendered.

Frozen hash browns and tater tots are another air fryer breakfast staple, and they come out significantly crispier than oven-baked versions. Spread them in a single layer, leaving small gaps between pieces, and cook at 400°F (205°C) for 12 to 16 minutes, shaking the basket halfway through. If you're making hash brown patties rather than shredded or tot-style, flip them once around the 8-minute mark for even browning on both sides. For homemade shredded hash browns, squeeze out as much moisture as possible from the grated potato before forming patties, since excess moisture is the main reason homemade hash browns turn soft instead of crisp.

Eggs are trickier in an air fryer than most other breakfast foods, but a few methods work well. For a baked egg, crack an egg directly into a small ramekin or silicone muffin cup, season it, and cook at 320°F (160°C) for about 6 to 8 minutes for a soft-set yolk, or up to 10 minutes for a firmer yolk. Hard-boiled eggs also work surprisingly well: place whole eggs directly in the basket and cook at 270°F (132°C) for 15 to 17 minutes, then transfer immediately to an ice bath. This method produces shells that peel more easily than stovetop boiling for many people, though results can vary by egg freshness.

Frozen breakfast sandwiches and burritos reheat far better in an air fryer than in a microwave, which tends to leave the bread soggy while the inside stays cold in spots. Wrap the sandwich loosely in foil for the first few minutes if it has a delicate pastry or biscuit component, then unwrap for the last couple of minutes to crisp the exterior. A typical frozen breakfast sandwich takes about 8 to 10 minutes at 360°F (182°C), while a larger frozen burrito may need 12 to 15 minutes, flipped once halfway through, with a quick check to make sure the center is heated through.

French toast sticks, whether homemade or frozen, are one of the easiest wins for an air fryer breakfast. For frozen sticks, arrange them in a single layer and cook at 380°F (193°C) for 8 to 10 minutes, flipping halfway, until golden and warmed through. For homemade sticks, dip thick bread slices in a standard egg-and-milk mixture, cut into strips, and cook at 360°F (182°C) for about 10 minutes, flipping once, until the exterior is golden and slightly crisp while the inside stays custardy. A light spray of oil or butter on the sticks before cooking helps them brown more evenly.

Pastries like frozen biscuits, croissants, and toaster pastries also do well in an air fryer, often coming out flakier than they would from a toaster or microwave. Frozen biscuits typically need 10 to 14 minutes at 330°F (165°C), since a lower, gentler temperature helps them rise properly before the outside sets. Croissants, whether fresh from the bakery case or frozen and thawed, crisp up nicely at 330°F (165°C) for 4 to 6 minutes, just enough to warm through and re-crisp the layers without drying out the inside.

If you're cooking breakfast for more than one person, a dual-zone or larger-capacity air fryer becomes especially useful, since you can run bacon in one basket while hash browns or a breakfast sandwich cook in the other, finishing everything at roughly the same time instead of doing multiple sequential batches in a single small basket. Even with a single-basket model, a smart sequencing approach, like starting with hash browns since they take longest, then bacon, then a quick reheat of pastries while everything else stays warm, can get a full breakfast on the table in under 25 minutes total.

A few general tips apply across nearly all air fryer breakfast foods. Always preheat for a few minutes when you're after a crisp texture, since starting in a cold basket leads to paler, softer results across the board. Don't stack items unless a recipe specifically calls for a rack accessory, since breakfast foods like hash browns and pastries need direct airflow on top and bottom to crisp evenly. And keep a close eye on anything with sugar or syrup glazes, since these can burn quickly at higher temperatures; when in doubt, drop the temperature by 20 degrees and add a couple of minutes rather than risking a scorched glaze.

If breakfast is one of the main reasons you're using your air fryer, look for a model with a wide, shallow basket or multiple zones, since breakfast foods tend to need more single-layer surface area relative to their volume than dinner items like proteins. A larger-capacity unit also makes it realistic to cook for a family without multiple rounds. For help comparing basket shapes, capacities, and dual-zone options, take a look at HomePilot's air fryer guide and our roundup of top picks.

Sausage, whether links, patties, or precooked breakfast sausage, rounds out an air fryer breakfast nicely and follows similar principles to bacon. Raw sausage links cook at 380°F (193°C) for 10 to 14 minutes, turning once or twice to brown evenly on all sides, while precooked sausage links or patties just need 6 to 8 minutes at 360°F (182°C) to heat through and crisp the casing or exterior. As with bacon, the rendered fat drips away from the food during cooking, which means sausage comes out a little less greasy than pan-frying while still developing good color. If you're cooking sausage alongside other breakfast items in a single-basket model, sausage is a good candidate to cook first or last depending on whether you want its drippings to flavor the basket for subsequent items or keep things separate.

Smoothie bowls, yogurt parfaits, and overnight oats obviously don't involve the air fryer directly, but a warm topping cooked in the air fryer can elevate any of these cold breakfast options. A quick batch of granola, made by tossing rolled oats with a little oil, honey, and cinnamon and air frying at 300°F (150°C) for 8 to 10 minutes with a stir halfway through, gives you a warm, crunchy topping in less time than an oven-baked batch and without heating up the kitchen. This kind of small-batch approach, where the air fryer handles just one component of a larger breakfast, is often the most practical way to fit it into a morning routine that also includes cold or no-cook elements.

faq.question: Can you cook bacon in an air fryer without it splattering everywhere?
faq.answer: Yes, bacon cooked at 380°F (193°C) for 8 to 12 minutes in a single layer renders its fat into the drawer below, eliminating stovetop splatter and making cleanup as simple as wiping out the drawer.

faq.question: How do you make crispy hash browns in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Spread frozen hash browns in a single layer with small gaps and cook at 400°F (205°C) for 12 to 16 minutes, shaking halfway. For homemade versions, squeeze out as much moisture as possible before cooking.

faq.question: Can you cook eggs in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Yes. Crack an egg into a ramekin and cook at 320°F (160°C) for 6 to 8 minutes for a soft yolk, or place whole eggs in the basket at 270°F (132°C) for 15 to 17 minutes for hard-boiled eggs.

faq.question: How do you reheat a frozen breakfast sandwich in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Wrap it loosely in foil for the first few minutes, then unwrap for the last couple of minutes to crisp the exterior. Most sandwiches take about 8 to 10 minutes at 360°F (182°C).

title: How to Cook Pork Chops in an Air Fryer
description: Cook juicy, never-dry pork chops in an air fryer with this guide covering brining, breading options, temperatures, and exact times by thickness.
author: Maya Hart
body: Pork chops have a reputation for drying out, and that reputation is mostly earned, since pork chops are a lean cut that goes from juicy to chalky in a fairly narrow window of overcooking. The good news is that an air fryer's fast, even heat actually makes it easier to hit that window consistently than a stovetop or oven, as long as you account for thickness, starting temperature, and the fact that pork doesn't need to be cooked nearly as long as older recipes suggest. Modern food safety guidance considers pork safe at 145°F (63°C) with a brief rest, which is well below the well-done texture many people grew up associating with pork chops.

Thickness is the most important variable, more important than weight or cut, because it directly determines cook time. Thin chops, around half an inch, cook the fastest but are also the easiest to overcook, since there's very little buffer between done and dry. Thick-cut chops, an inch to an inch and a half, are actually more forgiving in an air fryer because the exterior has time to brown nicely while the thicker interior stays juicy. If you have a choice at the store, bone-in chops around an inch thick are a great middle ground for air frying, since the bone helps insulate the meat near it and slows cooking slightly in that area, helping prevent overcooking right at the bone.

Bringing your chops to room temperature for about 15 to 20 minutes before cooking helps them cook more evenly, since a chop straight from the refrigerator has a colder center that needs more time to come up to temperature, during which the exterior can overcook. This is a small step but it meaningfully reduces the gap between when the outside looks done and when the inside actually reaches a safe temperature, which is the gap that produces dry pork.

For plain seasoned chops, pat them dry, rub both sides with oil, and season generously with salt, pepper, and whatever spices you like, garlic powder, paprika, and dried thyme are a reliable combination. Preheat the air fryer to 400°F (205°C), then cook half-inch chops for 8 to 10 minutes, three-quarter-inch chops for 10 to 12 minutes, and full-inch chops for 12 to 15 minutes, flipping once at the halfway point. Bone-in chops generally need a minute or two more than boneless chops of the same thickness because of the bone's insulating effect.

A wet brine makes a noticeable difference if you have 30 minutes to spare, and an even bigger difference if you can plan an hour or more ahead. A simple brine of a quarter cup of salt dissolved in four cups of water, with a tablespoon of sugar if you like a touch of caramelization, will season the meat throughout and help it retain moisture during cooking. Rinse and pat the chops completely dry after brining, since wet meat won't brown properly, and skip additional salt in your seasoning blend since the brine has already seasoned the interior.

Breaded pork chops are another popular option and work well in an air fryer, producing a crisp, almost fried texture with very little oil. Use the standard flour, egg, and breadcrumb dredging process, pressing the breadcrumbs firmly onto the chop so they adhere through cooking. A light spray of oil over the breading before cooking is essential here; without it, the breadcrumbs will look dry and pale rather than golden. Cook breaded chops at 380°F (193°C) for 12 to 15 minutes for a half-inch to three-quarter-inch chop, flipping halfway through and giving the second side another light spray of oil if it looks dry.

An instant-read thermometer is the single most useful tool for pork chops, more so than for almost any other protein, because the visual cues for doneness are subtle and the difference between 145°F and 165°F is the difference between juicy and dry. Insert the thermometer into the thickest part of the chop, avoiding the bone if there is one, and pull the chops from the basket at 140 to 142°F (60-61°C). The temperature will continue to rise a few more degrees during the rest, a phenomenon called carryover cooking, landing you right at the recommended 145°F (63°C) by the time you cut into it.

Resting matters for pork chops just as it does for larger roasts, even though the rest time is shorter, around 3 to 5 minutes is enough. Tent the chops loosely with foil during this time. Cutting into a chop immediately after it comes out of the basket releases the juices that have been pushed toward the center during cooking, leaving you with a drier bite than if you'd waited those few extra minutes.

If your chops are coming out dry despite following times closely, the most likely culprits are cooking from cold straight out of the refrigerator, using chops that are thinner than the recipe assumed, or pulling them at too high a temperature because your air fryer runs hotter than its display indicates. If you've ruled out the first two, try pulling your next batch 5 degrees earlier than the thermometer target and see if that produces better results once carryover cooking is accounted for. Conversely, if chops come out looking pale and undercooked at the stated time, your unit may run cooler, and adding 2 to 3 minutes per side is a reasonable adjustment.

Pork chops are a great example of a food where having reliable temperature control and basket space for flipping really pays off, since you need enough room to maneuver tongs without crowding multiple chops together. If pork chops, breaded cutlets, and similar proteins are regular dinner items in your house, a model with a roomy basket and accurate digital temperature settings will make these techniques easier to apply consistently. For more on choosing a model suited to everyday protein cooking, check out HomePilot's air fryer guide and our comparison of top picks.

Marinades work well with pork chops too, and unlike a wet brine, a marinade adds flavor as well as some moisture retention, though the effect on juiciness is generally milder than a true brine. A marinade of olive oil, soy sauce, garlic, and a touch of brown sugar or honey for 30 minutes to a few hours gives chops a flavorful crust, but watch the cooking time closely if your marinade contains sugar, since sugary marinades brown and can burn faster than plain seasoned chops. If you're using a sweeter marinade, consider dropping the cooking temperature to 375°F (190°C) and adding a couple of minutes to the total time, which gives the sugars time to caramelize without scorching before the chop reaches a safe internal temperature.

Boneless pork chops and pork tenderloin medallions, which are sometimes confused with chops but come from a different, more tender cut, both benefit from the same general approach but cook a bit faster due to their more uniform shape and typically smaller size. Tenderloin medallions cut about an inch thick cook in roughly 8 to 10 minutes at 400°F (205°C), flipping once, and like chops should be pulled at 140 to 142°F (60-61°C) before resting. Because tenderloin is even leaner than loin chops, it's especially prone to drying out if overcooked, so if you're cooking tenderloin for the first time in your air fryer, lean toward checking it a minute or two earlier than you would a chop of similar thickness.

faq.question: What temperature should pork chops be cooked to in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Pull chops from the basket around 140 to 142°F (60-61°C) and let them rest for 3 to 5 minutes. Carryover cooking will bring them to the recommended safe temperature of 145°F (63°C).

faq.question: How long do pork chops take in an air fryer?
faq.answer: At 400°F (205°C), half-inch chops take 8 to 10 minutes, three-quarter-inch chops take 10 to 12 minutes, and full-inch chops take 12 to 15 minutes, flipping once halfway through.

faq.question: Why do air fryer pork chops come out dry?
faq.answer: The most common causes are cooking chops straight from the refrigerator, using thinner chops than the recipe assumed, or pulling them at too high an internal temperature. Brining beforehand also helps retain moisture.

faq.question: Should I bread pork chops before air frying?
faq.answer: Breading is optional but works well. Use a standard flour, egg, and breadcrumb dredge, spray the breading lightly with oil so it browns, and cook at 380°F (193°C) for 12 to 15 minutes, flipping halfway.

title: How to Reheat Leftovers in an Air Fryer
description: Reheat leftovers in an air fryer to restore restaurant-quality crispiness, with exact times and temperatures for pizza, fried food, fries, rice, and pasta.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: One of the most underrated uses for an air fryer has nothing to do with cooking food from scratch at all: it's bringing leftovers back to life. A microwave heats food quickly but does nothing for texture, often leaving crispy foods soggy and reheated proteins rubbery. An air fryer, by contrast, uses dry circulating heat that re-crisps exteriors while warming the inside, which is why leftover pizza, fried chicken, and fries taste dramatically closer to fresh out of an air fryer than out of a microwave. The trade-off is that reheating takes a few more minutes than zapping a plate, but for most foods that trade-off is well worth it.

Pizza is the food people are most surprised by. Place leftover slices directly in the basket, ideally not stacked, and reheat at 350°F (175°C) for 3 to 5 minutes. The crust crisps back up, the cheese remelts properly, and you avoid the soggy-bottomed, lukewarm-in-the-middle result that's so common with microwaved pizza. If your slices have a particularly thick or doughy crust, an extra minute or two helps the bottom catch up to the toppings. There's no need to add oil or cover the pizza; the dry heat is exactly what you want here.

Fried foods, including fried chicken, chicken tenders, fish fillets, and egg rolls, are where an air fryer reheats most dramatically better than any other method. These foods rely on a crisp exterior coating that a microwave turns soft and a regular oven can take a long time to restore. Reheat fried foods at 360 to 375°F (182-190°C) for 6 to 10 minutes depending on size, flipping halfway through. Larger pieces like bone-in fried chicken thighs need the longer end of that range and benefit from a check with a thermometer to confirm the inside is hot all the way through, since the goal is reheating, not just re-crisping the surface.

French fries and other crispy potato sides are nearly as dramatic a transformation. Spread leftover fries, tater tots, or potato wedges in a single layer and reheat at 380°F (193°C) for 4 to 6 minutes, shaking the basket once. Fries that have gone limp and pale in the refrigerator come out re-crisped and hot, often tasting closer to a fresh batch than you'd expect from something that was sitting in a takeout container a day earlier. Avoid microwaving fries beforehand even briefly, since that locks in moisture that the air fryer then has to work harder to remove.

Rice and grain-based leftovers need a slightly different approach since they have no crispy coating to restore and can dry out if treated like fried food. Spread rice or grains in a thin, even layer in the basket, drizzle with a small amount of water or oil to add back moisture, and reheat at 350°F (175°C) for 4 to 6 minutes, stirring or shaking once. This works well for fried rice especially, since the air fryer can actually add a pleasant slight crispness to the edges of the rice, something a microwave can never do.

Pasta dishes reheat reasonably well in an air fryer, though results depend on the sauce. Pasta in a thicker sauce, like baked ziti or a casserole-style dish, does well in an oven-safe dish placed in the basket at 350°F (175°C) for 8 to 12 minutes, covered loosely with foil for the first portion of the time to prevent the top from drying out, then uncovered for the last few minutes if you want a slightly crisp top layer. Pasta in a thin oil-based sauce can dry out more easily, so add a splash of water or extra oil before reheating and keep an eye on it, since these dishes generally need less time, around 5 to 8 minutes.

Baked goods like croissants, biscuits, and dinner rolls reheat beautifully at a lower temperature, around 300 to 325°F (150-165°C), for 3 to 5 minutes. This gentle heat warms the inside while re-crisping any flaky exterior layers without drying the whole item out, which is what tends to happen if you try to reheat pastries at higher temperatures meant for proteins. Cookies and other soft baked goods generally don't need an air fryer at all and reheat fine in short bursts in the microwave, so save the air fryer for items where texture really matters.

A few general principles apply across almost everything you reheat. Bring food closer to room temperature for 10 to 15 minutes before reheating if you have time, since cold food straight from the refrigerator takes longer and can over-crisp the outside before the inside catches up. Don't overcrowd the basket, since reheating relies on the same airflow principles as cooking from raw, and pieces that touch each other won't re-crisp evenly. And start checking a minute or two before the suggested time, since reheated portions are often smaller than a full cooking batch and can finish faster than you expect.

If you regularly reheat a variety of leftovers, especially mixed plates with both crispy and moist components, a dual-zone air fryer is particularly useful, since you can run a higher temperature for fried sides in one basket while a lower, gentler setting handles rice or vegetables in the other, bringing a full plate back to temperature at the same time. A basket or tray with a non-stick, easy-to-clean surface also matters more for reheating than for fresh cooking, since reheated sauces and cheese can be sticky. For help comparing models with these features, take a look at HomePilot's air fryer guide and our roundup of top picks.

Vegetables and side dishes that were originally roasted or air-fried tend to reheat almost as well as they cooked the first time, since they're already designed to handle dry heat. Spread leftover roasted vegetables in a single layer and reheat at 375°F (190°C) for 4 to 6 minutes, shaking once; you may notice they brown even further on this second pass, which many people actually prefer. Steamed or boiled vegetables, on the other hand, have more retained moisture and can turn mushy if reheated the same way, so if you're warming up something like steamed broccoli or green beans, a shorter time at a lower temperature, around 350°F (175°C) for 3 to 4 minutes, is enough to take the chill off without overcooking already-tender vegetables further.

Soups, stews, and other liquid-based leftovers are the one category that doesn't belong in an air fryer at all, since the basket has no way to contain liquid and pouring soup into a perforated basket would simply drain through to the drawer below. For these dishes, stick with a microwave or stovetop, and reserve your air fryer for the components that actually benefit from dry heat, like a side of crusty bread to go with the soup, which can be warmed and crisped at 350°F (175°C) for 2 to 3 minutes while the soup itself heats separately. Knowing which leftovers to route to the air fryer and which to handle conventionally is part of getting the most out of the appliance: it excels at restoring texture to anything that was originally crispy, baked, or roasted, but it isn't a replacement for every reheating task in the kitchen.

Sandwiches and wraps present a unique reheating challenge because they combine bread, fillings, and sometimes cold elements like lettuce or mayo that don't belong in a hot appliance. For a hot sandwich like a grilled cheese or a pulled pork sandwich, remove any cold toppings first, reheat the sandwich itself at 350°F (175°C) for 3 to 5 minutes to warm the filling and re-crisp the bread, then add the cold elements back afterward. This two-step approach takes only slightly longer than tossing the whole thing in a microwave, but the difference in the bread's texture, crisp rather than damp and chewy, makes it worth the extra minute, especially for anything with a toasted or buttered exterior.

faq.question: Is an air fryer better than a microwave for reheating leftovers?
faq.answer: For anything with a crispy texture, like pizza, fried chicken, or fries, yes. The air fryer's dry circulating heat re-crisps the exterior while warming the inside, while a microwave tends to make these foods soggy.

faq.question: How do you reheat pizza in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Place slices directly in the basket without stacking and reheat at 350°F (175°C) for 3 to 5 minutes. No oil or cover is needed, and the crust crisps back up while the cheese remelts.

faq.question: How do you reheat fried chicken without drying it out?
faq.answer: Reheat at 360 to 375°F (182-190°C) for 6 to 10 minutes depending on size, flipping halfway. Larger bone-in pieces need the longer end of the range and should be checked to ensure they're hot throughout.

faq.question: Can you reheat rice or pasta in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Yes. Spread rice or grains thin, add a splash of water or oil for moisture, and reheat at 350°F (175°C) for 4 to 6 minutes, stirring once. Saucy pasta dishes do well in an oven-safe dish covered loosely with foil.

title: How to Make Air Fryer Desserts
description: Make easy air fryer desserts at home, from cookies and cinnamon rolls to baked apples and churros, with temperatures and times for any basket size.
author: Maya Hart
body: Desserts might not be the first thing that comes to mind when you think of an air fryer, but it handles a surprising range of sweets well, especially small-batch and individually portioned treats. Because air fryers heat up faster than a conventional oven and work in smaller quantities, they're particularly good for satisfying a dessert craving without committing to a full-size cake or baking a dozen cookies you'll be tempted to eat all at once. The key adjustment to keep in mind across almost all air fryer desserts is that baked goods are more sensitive to temperature than savory foods, so gentler heat and closer monitoring matter more here than anywhere else in your air fryer repertoire.

Cookies are one of the easiest places to start. You can bake cookie dough directly in the basket, either dropped by the spoonful onto parchment paper cut to fit, or pressed into a small oven-safe pan or silicone mold if your dough is very soft. Bake at 320 to 330°F (160-165°C) for 6 to 8 minutes for a standard drop cookie, checking at the lower end since cookies can go from soft-set to overdone quickly in the concentrated heat of a small basket. Leave more space between cookies than you would on a baking sheet, since air fryer cookies tend to spread more due to the intense, direct heat from above.

Baked apples are a low-effort dessert that air fryers handle particularly well. Core whole apples, leaving the bottom intact to hold filling, and fill the centers with a mixture of brown sugar, cinnamon, oats, and a pat of butter. Cook at 350°F (175°C) for 15 to 20 minutes depending on the size and variety of apple, firmer apples like Granny Smith or Honeycrisp hold their shape better and may need the full 20 minutes, while softer varieties cook faster and can turn mushy if left too long. The apples are done when a knife slides in easily but the fruit still holds its shape.

Cinnamon rolls, whether from a tube of refrigerated dough or homemade, bake well in an air fryer, often with better browning on top than a conventional oven produces. Arrange the rolls with a little extra space between them compared to a baking pan, since they'll expand as they cook and rolls that are too close together can fuse into one another in a way that's harder to separate cleanly afterward. Bake at 320°F (160°C) for 8 to 12 minutes, checking the centers with a toothpick. Add the icing or glaze after baking and a brief cooling period, since icing applied before baking will simply melt and run off rather than setting on top.

Churros are a fun project that air fryers handle better than you might expect, producing a result closer to baked churros than deep-fried ones, with a crisp exterior but a slightly less greasy overall texture. Pipe choux pastry dough into strips directly onto parchment, then transfer to the basket and cook at 375°F (190°C) for 10 to 12 minutes until golden and firm to the touch. While still warm, toss the churros in a cinnamon-sugar mixture so it adheres to the slightly sticky exterior; waiting until they've fully cooled means the sugar won't stick as well.

Brownies and small cakes can be made in an air fryer using a small oven-safe pan or ramekins that fit inside your basket. Because air fryers run hot and the pan sits close to the heating element, reduce your recipe's stated oven temperature by about 25°F (15°C) and check for doneness a few minutes earlier than the original recipe suggests. A standard brownie batter in a 6 to 7 inch pan typically takes 20 to 25 minutes at 320°F (160°C), and a toothpick inserted in the center should come out with a few moist crumbs rather than fully clean, since brownies continue to set as they cool.

Fruit-based desserts beyond baked apples also work well, including banana boats, where a banana is split lengthwise, filled with chocolate chips and mini marshmallows, wrapped in foil, and cooked at 375°F (190°C) for 8 to 10 minutes until the filling is melted and gooey. Grilled or roasted pineapple slices, brushed with a little butter and brown sugar, caramelize nicely at 380°F (193°C) for 6 to 8 minutes, flipping once, and make a simple dessert or topping for ice cream with almost no prep.

A few things go wrong more often with desserts than with savory foods, so it's worth watching for them specifically. Sugar and glazes burn faster than you'd expect, especially on items positioned near the top of the basket close to the heating element, so if you're making something with a sugary topping, check it a couple of minutes earlier than you would an unsweetened version of the same recipe. Parchment paper is your friend for sticky doughs and batters, but make sure it's weighted down by the food itself before the fan turns on, since loose parchment can blow around and even contact the heating element in some models. And because most air fryer desserts are small-batch by nature, resist the urge to double a recipe in the same basket; portions that are too crowded won't bake evenly and the centers can stay raw while the edges burn.

If desserts are something you want to make regularly, an air fryer with a larger capacity or an oven-style design gives you more room for small baking pans, ramekins, and trays without everything touching the heating element. A model with a clear viewing window is also genuinely useful for desserts, since you can keep an eye on browning without opening the basket and losing heat, which can affect rising baked goods. To compare capacity and features across models suited to baking small treats, check out HomePilot's air fryer guide and our roundup of top picks.

Hand pies and turnovers, made from store-bought puff pastry or pie dough folded around a fruit or sweet filling, are another strong option for the air fryer, producing flakier results than a microwave and faster results than a full oven. Brush the assembled pies lightly with an egg wash or milk before cooking to encourage browning, and cut a small slit in the top to let steam escape so the filling doesn't burst through the seams. Cook at 350°F (175°C) for 12 to 15 minutes, checking that the pastry is golden brown and the filling is bubbling slightly at the vents. Let hand pies cool for at least 5 minutes before eating, since fruit fillings retain heat and can be surprisingly hot immediately after cooking, even when the pastry itself feels only warm.

Doughnuts, whether from refrigerated biscuit dough cut into rings or a yeasted dough you've proofed yourself, are a popular air fryer project because they come out closer to baked doughnuts than fried ones, with a lighter texture and noticeably less oil. Cook at 350°F (175°C) for 5 to 7 minutes, flipping halfway through, until golden on both sides. While still warm, brush them with melted butter and toss in cinnamon sugar, or dip in a simple glaze once they've cooled slightly so the glaze sets rather than soaking in completely. Because doughnuts rise and expand somewhat during cooking, leave more space between them than you think you'll need, since rings that touch can fuse together at the edges and end up with a doughy, unbrowned seam where they met.

faq.question: Can you bake cookies in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Yes. Bake drop cookies on parchment at 320 to 330°F (160-165°C) for 6 to 8 minutes, leaving extra space between cookies since they spread more under the concentrated heat than they would in an oven.

faq.question: How do you make baked apples in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Core whole apples, fill with a brown sugar, cinnamon, oats, and butter mixture, and cook at 350°F (175°C) for 15 to 20 minutes depending on the apple's size and firmness.

faq.question: Do I need to adjust oven recipes for brownies or cakes in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Yes, lower the oven temperature by about 25°F (15°C) and check for doneness a few minutes earlier than the recipe states, since air fryers run hotter relative to their setting in a small enclosed space.

faq.question: Why does sugar or icing burn in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Sugary toppings are positioned close to the heating element and can scorch quickly. Add icing or glaze after baking rather than before, and check sweet items a couple of minutes earlier than unsweetened versions.

title: How to Cook Vegetables Without Drying Them Out
description: Stop dry, shriveled air fryer vegetables with this guide on oil ratios, cut sizes, temperatures, and timing tricks for keeping veggies tender inside.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: If you've air-fried vegetables and ended up with something closer to vegetable chips than the tender-crisp roasted vegetables you were hoping for, the issue almost always comes down to a handful of fixable variables: oil quantity, cut size, temperature, and timing. Vegetables have a much higher water content than most other foods you cook in an air fryer, and that water is exactly what gives them their texture; cook off too much of it and you're left with shriveled, leathery results no matter how good your seasoning is. The goal with most vegetables is a surface that's lightly browned and slightly crisp while the interior stays tender and moist, and getting there reliably comes down to understanding how each variable affects that balance.

Cut size is the first lever, and it's more important for vegetables than for almost anything else you cook. Smaller pieces have more surface area relative to their volume, which means they lose moisture faster and are more prone to drying out before they're tender inside. For vegetables you want to stay tender, like broccoli, cauliflower, or Brussels sprouts, cut pieces on the larger side, roughly one and a half to two inches, rather than small florets that will dry out at the edges long before the centers soften. For vegetables where you actually want a drier, more intensely roasted result, like kale chips, smaller and thinner pieces are appropriate, but that's the exception rather than the rule.

Oil is doing more work than just flavor and browning; it's also slowing moisture loss by coating the vegetable's surface, which is part of why vegetables cooked with too little oil tend to dry out and shrivel rather than roast. A light but even coating, around a tablespoon or a tablespoon and a half per pound of vegetables, tossed thoroughly so every piece is coated, makes a noticeable difference. The mistake to avoid is the opposite extreme too: vegetables sitting in a pool of oil at the bottom of the basket will steam in that oil rather than roast, and the parts in contact with the pooled oil often turn soggy rather than crisp.

Temperature matters because vegetables cooked too hot for too long dry out from the outside in before the inside is properly tender, especially with denser vegetables like carrots, potatoes, or whole green beans. For most vegetables, 375 to 400°F (190-205°C) is the sweet spot, hot enough to brown the surface and develop flavor through caramelization, but not so hot that the exterior dries to a crisp shell before the inside softens. Leafy and delicate vegetables like asparagus tips, spinach, or thinly sliced zucchini do better at the lower end of that range, or even down to 350°F (175°C), since they have less mass and can overcook in the time it takes denser vegetables to properly roast.

Time is where a lot of dryness problems actually originate, often because people default to the longer end of a suggested range out of caution about undercooking. Most vegetables cut into reasonable pieces are done in 12 to 18 minutes at 375 to 400°F, and checking at the earlier end of that window, then adding time in 2-minute increments if needed, prevents the overcooking that happens when you commit to a full 20-plus minutes upfront. A fork or knife tip should slide into the vegetable with slight resistance, not glide through with no resistance at all, which is a sign of overcooking, and not meet hard resistance, which means it needs more time.

Shaking or tossing the basket partway through cooking does more than just promote even browning; it also redistributes any moisture that's collected at the bottom of the basket back over the vegetables, which can help prevent the bottom layer from drying out faster than the top. For most vegetable batches, one shake at the halfway point is enough, but for vegetables cut into a mix of sizes, like a medley of bell peppers, onions, and zucchini that naturally vary in density, two shakes spaced more evenly through the cook can help everything finish closer to the same time.

Some vegetables genuinely benefit from a brief par-cook before air frying, particularly dense root vegetables like whole carrots, beets, or large potato chunks that would otherwise need a very long air fry time to become tender, by which point the exterior would be well past done. A quick steam or microwave for 3 to 5 minutes before air frying softens the interior partway, so the air fryer's job becomes mostly about browning the exterior rather than cooking the vegetable through from scratch, which keeps the total air fryer time short enough that drying isn't a concern.

Vegetables with naturally high water content, like zucchini, mushrooms, and tomatoes, present a different challenge: they release moisture as they cook, which can create steam that prevents browning even though the vegetable itself isn't drying out. For these, avoid overcrowding more than usual, since the released moisture needs somewhere to go, and consider salting watery vegetables like zucchini and letting them sit for 10 to 15 minutes before cooking, then patting them dry, which draws out some of that excess water before it ever hits the basket.

If you're finishing vegetables with a sauce, glaze, or grated cheese, add it during the last few minutes of cooking rather than at the start. Adding wet ingredients too early traps moisture against the vegetable's surface for the entire cook time, working against everything else you're doing to get a good texture. A balsamic glaze drizzled on roasted Brussels sprouts, or parmesan sprinkled over asparagus, both work best added with about 2 to 3 minutes left, just enough time for the topping to set without steaming the vegetable underneath.

Because vegetables benefit so much from single-layer spacing and good airflow, basket shape and size matter more here than for many other foods. A wide, shallow basket lets you fit a full pound of vegetables in roughly one layer, while a deep, narrow basket often forces vegetables into a pile that steams at the bottom regardless of how careful you are with oil and timing. If vegetables are a regular part of your meals, a larger or dual-zone air fryer gives you the surface area to cook a full side dish without crowding, often alongside a protein in the second zone. For more on choosing a model with the right basket dimensions for vegetable-heavy cooking, browse HomePilot's air fryer guide and our comparison of top picks.

Frozen vegetables behave somewhat differently from fresh ones when it comes to drying out, mainly because the freezing and thawing process has already broken down some of the cell structure that holds moisture in fresh produce. Frozen vegetables release moisture quickly once they hit the hot basket, which is why they often steam rather than roast in the first several minutes of cooking. Don't add oil to frozen vegetables right away; instead, cook them plain for the first 8 to 10 minutes at 380°F (193°C) to let the released moisture cook off, then toss with a small amount of oil and seasoning and continue cooking for another 5 to 8 minutes to finish browning. Adding oil at the start with frozen vegetables tends to create a greasy slurry at the bottom of the basket rather than helping the vegetables crisp.

Finally, consider how you're cutting vegetables in relation to their natural shape and grain, not just their overall size. Vegetables cut along their natural fibers, like carrots or zucchini sliced lengthwise into batons, tend to hold their structure better and dry out less than the same vegetable cut into small cubes, which expose more cut surfaces to the hot air from multiple directions. For vegetables like bell peppers or onions that you're cutting into strips for a stir-fry style dish, cutting with the grain rather than against it can mean the difference between strips that stay tender-crisp and ones that curl and dry at the edges before the rest of the piece has cooked through. This is a small detail, but it compounds with the other factors covered here, oil, temperature, time, and spacing, to determine whether your vegetables come out the way you intended.

faq.question: Why do my air fryer vegetables come out shriveled and dry?
faq.answer: This usually happens from cutting pieces too small, using too little oil, cooking at too high a temperature for too long, or simply running the full suggested time when the vegetables were already done earlier.

faq.question: How much oil should I use for air fryer vegetables?
faq.answer: About one to one and a half tablespoons per pound, tossed evenly over all the pieces. This light coating slows moisture loss and helps browning without pooling at the bottom of the basket and causing sogginess.

faq.question: What temperature is best for roasting vegetables in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Most vegetables do well at 375 to 400°F (190-205°C) for 12 to 18 minutes. Delicate vegetables like asparagus or thin zucchini do better at 350°F (175°C) since they cook faster and can dry out at higher heat.

faq.question: Should I par-cook dense vegetables before air frying?
faq.answer: For very dense items like whole carrots or large potato chunks, a 3 to 5 minute steam or microwave beforehand softens the interior so the air fryer mainly needs to brown the exterior, preventing an overly long, drying cook time.

title: Air Fryer Meal Prep Ideas
description: Air fryer meal prep ideas to batch-cook proteins, vegetables, and grains for the week, keeping crispy texture without sogginess on reheat.
author: Maya Hart
body: Meal prepping with an air fryer is one of the fastest ways to turn a couple of hours on Sunday into five days of fast, decent dinners. The appliance's main advantages for batch cooking are speed, minimal preheating, and the ability to crisp foods without drowning them in oil, which means reheated leftovers actually taste like they were just cooked rather than soggy or steam-table limp. If you have been treating your air fryer as a one-meal-at-a-time gadget, shifting to a prep mindset changes the math: you are cooking once and eating four or five times, and the texture holds up far better than microwave-only leftovers.

Start with proteins, because they are the backbone of most prepped meals and the air fryer handles them efficiently. Boneless chicken thighs and breasts, seasoned simply with salt, pepper, and a neutral oil, cook in roughly 18 to 22 minutes at 380 to 400 degrees Fahrenheit, depending on thickness, and you can usually fit four to six pieces in a 6-quart basket without overlapping too much. Salmon fillets take about 10 to 12 minutes and reheat surprisingly well if you slightly undercook them the first time, since they will finish cooking during reheating. Ground meat patties, meatballs, and tofu cubes are also excellent prep candidates because they freeze and reheat cleanly, holding their shape and crust far better than oven-baked versions reheated in a microwave.

Vegetables are where the air fryer earns its keep for prep cooking. Broccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, bell peppers, zucchini, and sweet potato cubes all roast in 12 to 18 minutes with a light coat of oil and salt, and because the basket design circulates hot air around each piece, you get actual browning and caramelization rather than the steamed texture you often get from sheet-pan roasting in a crowded oven. The trick for prep is to slightly undercook vegetables on the first pass, by about 2 minutes less than you would for immediate eating, since they will get a second blast of heat when you reheat them later in the week and you do not want them to turn mushy.

Grains and starches round out the meal and are usually best cooked using your stovetop or rice cooker rather than the air fryer itself, but the air fryer becomes useful again when you want to reheat them with texture. Cooked rice, quinoa, or even leftover roasted potatoes can be tossed back into the basket for 4 to 6 minutes at 350 degrees to crisp up edges that would otherwise go gummy in a microwave. This is particularly effective for fried rice-style dishes, where a quick air fryer reheat brings back some of that wok-charred texture that microwaving completely flattens.

Batching by flavor profile rather than by ingredient makes the week more enjoyable and reduces decision fatigue. Instead of cooking five identical chicken breasts with the same seasoning, divide your protein into two or three flavor groups: a Mediterranean batch with lemon, oregano, and garlic; a Tex-Mex batch with cumin, chili powder, and lime; and a simple salt-and-pepper batch that works with almost any sauce you add later. Cook each batch separately since seasoning blends with strong spices like chili powder or smoked paprika can transfer flavor to the basket and affect the next batch if you do not wipe it down between runs.

Container choice matters more than people expect. Glass containers with vented lids are ideal because they let cooked food cool slightly before sealing, which reduces condensation and sogginess, and they go straight from the fridge into the air fryer for reheating without the plastic-warping concerns of some takeout containers. If you are prepping for five days, portion proteins and vegetables into separate compartments rather than mixing everything together, since different foods often need different reheat times and temperatures, and keeping them separate until the final reheat preserves texture better.

Reheating is really where air fryer meal prep separates itself from oven or microwave prep. Most cooked proteins and vegetables reheat well at 350 to 375 degrees Fahrenheit for 4 to 8 minutes, depending on portion size and how cold the food is coming out of the fridge. A useful habit is to reheat proteins and starches first for a few minutes, then add the vegetables for the final 2 to 3 minutes, since vegetables generally need less time and can dry out or burn if they are in the basket for the full reheat cycle. Spritzing a little water or oil on items that tend to dry out, like chicken breast or rice, helps restore moisture during reheating.

Capacity planning depends heavily on your air fryer's basket size and your household size. A compact 2 to 4-quart unit is fine for prepping meals for one person, but batch cooking for two or more people is much more efficient with a 6-quart or larger basket, or a dual-zone model that lets you cook two different foods simultaneously, like a protein in one zone and vegetables in the other, finishing both at the same time. If you are prepping in smaller batches throughout the week rather than one big Sunday session, even a compact air fryer can keep up, since each cook cycle is short enough to fit into a weekday routine.

One mistake that undermines a lot of meal prep is overcooking everything to the same doneness as a fresh meal, then reheating it again later, which effectively cooks the food twice and dries it out. Aim for proteins and vegetables that are about 90 percent done when you pull them for storage, planning on the reheat cycle to finish the job. This single adjustment makes a noticeable difference in how prepped meals taste by midweek, when reheated chicken or roasted vegetables can otherwise start to feel rubbery or leathery.

If your current air fryer struggles to keep up with weekly batch cooking, whether because the basket is too small, it takes multiple rounds to cook enough food, or it runs unevenly and leaves some pieces undercooked while others burn, it might be worth comparing models built with larger capacities or multiple cooking zones designed for exactly this kind of workflow. Our guide to the best air fryers breaks down which models handle larger batches most efficiently, and the broader air fryers guide covers capacity, wattage, and basket design considerations that matter most when you are cooking for the whole week rather than a single plate.

Sauces and dressings deserve their own prep step, since they are what keeps reheated proteins and vegetables from tasting dry or one-note by day four. Make two or three sauces at the start of the week, such as a yogurt-based herb sauce, a simple vinaigrette, and a soy-based glaze, and store them separately in small jars rather than tossing food in sauce ahead of time. Adding sauce just before eating, rather than during prep, keeps proteins from getting soggy in storage and lets you mix and match the same base proteins and vegetables into different-tasting meals throughout the week, which goes a long way toward preventing the boredom that derails most meal prep routines by Wednesday.

Labeling matters more than it seems like it should, especially once you have several similar-looking containers in the fridge with different seasoning blends. A simple piece of masking tape with the protein, flavor profile, and the date it was cooked saves time during the week and helps you track which containers should be eaten first. This is particularly useful if you are prepping in two flavor batches, since a Tex-Mex chicken thigh and a lemon-herb chicken thigh can look nearly identical once cooked, and grabbing the wrong one is a minor but avoidable annoyance during a rushed weekday lunch.

faq.question: How long does air fryer meal prep stay fresh in the fridge?
faq.answer: Most cooked proteins, grains, and vegetables stay fresh for 3 to 5 days when stored in airtight containers in the refrigerator. For longer storage, freeze portions and reheat directly from frozen, adding a few extra minutes to the air fryer cycle.

faq.question: Should I season food before or after freezing for meal prep?
faq.answer: Season before cooking and freezing, since most spice blends hold up well through freezing and reheating. Save fresh additions like herbs, citrus, or sauces for after reheating, as these can lose flavor or become watery when frozen.

faq.question: Can I cook multiple foods at once for faster meal prep?
faq.answer: Yes, if your air fryer has a dual-zone or multi-basket design, you can cook two different foods simultaneously at different temperatures and times. With a single-basket model, cook items with similar temperatures together, but keep strong-smelling ingredients separate to avoid flavor transfer.

faq.question: Why do my meal-prepped vegetables turn soggy when reheated?
faq.answer: This usually happens when vegetables are fully cooked the first time and then reheated, effectively cooking them twice. Slightly undercook vegetables during the initial prep session so the reheating cycle finishes them without making them mushy.

title: How to Cook Frozen Burgers in an Air Fryer
description: Learn how to cook frozen burgers in an air fryer for a juicy, evenly cooked patty with a seared crust, no thawing or stovetop mess required.
author: HomePilot Editorial Team
body: Cooking frozen burgers in an air fryer is one of the most reliable shortcuts in home cooking, because it solves the two biggest problems with frozen patties on a stovetop or grill: uneven cooking and a smoky kitchen. The air fryer's circulating hot air cooks the patty through while developing a browned crust on both sides, and because the basket lets fat drip away from the meat, you avoid the greasy splatter and flare-ups that come with pan-frying frozen beef. No thawing, no oil, and minimal cleanup make this one of the most-used functions for anyone who keeps a stack of frozen patties on hand for quick dinners.

Start by preheating the air fryer to 360 degrees Fahrenheit for about 3 minutes, though many machines cook frozen burgers just fine without preheating if you add an extra minute or two to the total time. Place the frozen patties directly in the basket in a single layer, leaving space between them so air can circulate around each one. Do not stack patties or overlap edges, since this creates pockets where the center stays frozen while the edges overcook. If you are cooking for a crowd and your basket is small, cook in batches rather than crowding everything in at once.

For a standard quarter-pound frozen patty, cook for 9 to 11 minutes total, flipping halfway through at around the 5-minute mark. Thinner fast-food-style patties, often around an eighth of a pound, need closer to 7 to 8 minutes total, while thicker pub-style patties, especially anything over a third of a pound, can take 12 to 14 minutes. The flip is important not just for even browning but because it helps the patty release any fat that has pooled underneath, which reduces smoke and helps both sides develop a better crust.

Internal temperature is the only reliable way to know a burger is done, especially with frozen patties where visual cues like color can be misleading. The USDA recommends ground beef reach an internal temperature of 160 degrees Fahrenheit, and a quick-read thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the patty is the most accurate check. If you pull a patty and it reads under 160, return it to the basket for another minute or two rather than guessing based on appearance, since frozen patties can look done on the outside while still being undercooked in the center.

Cheese is easy to add without extra equipment. About 1 minute before the burgers are finished cooking, open the basket, place a slice of cheese on each patty, and close it again. The residual heat and final minute of cooking will melt the cheese without overcooking the meat further. If your air fryer has a particularly strong top heating element, you can also reduce the temperature slightly for that final minute to prevent the cheese from scorching before it fully melts.

Seasoning frozen patties is more limited than seasoning fresh ground beef, since most pre-formed frozen burgers already come seasoned and the frozen surface does not hold loose seasoning well at the start of cooking. However, you can still improve the result by adding a light dusting of garlic powder, onion powder, smoked paprika, or black pepper after the first flip, once the surface has thawed slightly and developed some moisture that helps seasoning stick. Avoid adding salt before cooking, since it can draw moisture out of the surface and slow browning.

Turkey, chicken, plant-based, and bison patties all work in the air fryer using a similar approach, but timing varies. Frozen turkey and chicken patties generally need 10 to 14 minutes and should reach an internal temperature of 165 degrees Fahrenheit due to poultry safety guidelines. Plant-based patties, including pea-protein and soy-based burgers, typically cook faster, often in 8 to 10 minutes, and benefit from a light oil spray since they can dry out more easily than beef. Always check the package instructions as a starting point, since formulations vary significantly between brands, and adjust based on your specific air fryer's tendency to run hot or cool.

One detail that trips people up is basket liners and parchment paper. While these can help with cleanup, frozen burgers release a fair amount of fat as they cook, and a solid liner without holes can trap that fat underneath the patty, leading to a steamed rather than crisped bottom. If you use a liner, choose a perforated one designed for air fryers, or skip it entirely for burgers and rely on the basket's natural drainage design, then wipe down the basket afterward.

Toasting the buns at the same time is a simple way to round out the meal without dirtying another pan. During the last 1 to 2 minutes of the burger's cook time, place the bun halves cut-side down on top of the patties or, if there is room, along the edges of the basket. Watch them closely, since buns can go from toasted to burnt quickly given how much hotter air fryers run compared to a traditional toaster.

If you regularly cook frozen burgers and find that your current air fryer struggles to fit more than one or two patties at a time, runs unevenly with hot spots that burn one side while leaving the other pale, or takes noticeably longer than the times above, it may be a sign your machine's basket size or heating element is undersized for how you actually cook. Larger baskets and dual-zone designs make a real difference for burger nights with multiple people, and our guide to the best air fryers compares capacity and even-cooking performance across popular models, while the broader air fryers guide covers what wattage and basket shapes tend to perform best for foods like frozen patties.

If you are cooking burgers for a crowd and need to keep finished patties warm while later batches cook, resist the temptation to stack them on a plate covered with foil, since trapped steam will soften the crust you just built. Instead, place cooked patties on a wire rack set over a plate, which allows air to circulate underneath and keeps the bottom from steaming. If you have an oven available, setting it to its lowest temperature, often called a warm setting around 170 to 200 degrees Fahrenheit, and placing the rack inside keeps burgers at a safe serving temperature without continuing to cook them or drying them out before everyone is ready to eat.

Smash-style frozen patties, which are thinner and designed to develop a heavily browned, lacy edge, behave a bit differently in an air fryer than thicker patties. Because they are thin, they cook faster, often in 6 to 8 minutes total, and benefit from a slightly higher temperature around 380 degrees Fahrenheit to encourage browning before the inside overcooks. Flip these patties earlier, around the 3-minute mark, since their thinness means they reach the safe internal temperature quickly and additional time mostly serves to develop more crust rather than to finish cooking the center.

Condiments and toppings can also be prepared while the burgers cook, which is one of the underrated time savings of this method compared to stovetop cooking, where you typically need to stand at the pan the whole time. Because the air fryer runs unattended once it is set, you can use those 9 to 11 minutes to slice tomatoes, onions, and pickles, toast buns separately if your basket does not have room, or assemble a quick sauce, so that everything is ready the moment the burgers come out and the cheese has melted.

faq.question: Do I need to thaw burgers before air frying them?
faq.answer: No, frozen patties can go directly into the air fryer basket from the freezer. Cooking from frozen actually helps the patty hold its shape better than starting from a partially thawed state.

faq.question: What temperature should I set the air fryer for frozen burgers?
faq.answer: Set the air fryer to 360 degrees Fahrenheit for most quarter-pound frozen beef patties, cooking for 9 to 11 minutes and flipping halfway through. Thinner or thicker patties will need slightly less or more time.

faq.question: How do I know when a frozen burger is fully cooked?
faq.answer: Use a quick-read meat thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the patty. Ground beef should reach 160 degrees Fahrenheit, while ground turkey and chicken patties should reach 165 degrees Fahrenheit.

faq.question: Can I cook multiple frozen burgers at once in an air fryer?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as the patties are arranged in a single layer without overlapping. If your basket is small, cook in batches to avoid uneven cooking caused by crowding.

title: How to Make Crispy Tofu and Vegetables Together
description: Learn how to make crispy tofu and vegetables together in an air fryer with the right prep, timing, and seasoning for evenly cooked results.
author: Maya Hart
body: Crispy tofu and roasted vegetables are one of the most popular air fryer combinations, and for good reason: both benefit from the same dry, circulating heat, both can be seasoned with similar flavor profiles, and together they make a complete plant-based meal in under 25 minutes. The challenge most people run into is that tofu and vegetables do not always cook at the same rate, and tofu in particular needs a bit more preparation than most vegetables to get genuinely crispy rather than just dried out. Getting both right at the same time comes down to prep, cut size, and timing them so they finish together.

The single most important step for crispy tofu is removing excess moisture before it ever goes near oil or seasoning.
